target.title: fix(telegram): prevent RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID by stripping mailto links and adding rich-message fallback
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves Telegram `/status` slash-command replies fail to deliver when `richMessages=true` is configured. The Gateway executes the slash command, but Telegram's `sendRichMessage` API rejects the payload with `RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID`, leaving the user with no response. Closes #96363 ## Changes - `extensions/telegram/src/format.ts`: remove `mailto:` from `isTelegramRichLinkHref` so auto-detected or explicit email links render as plain visible text instead of `` anchors that trigger Telegram rich-message email-entity validation failures. - `extensions/telegram/src/send.ts`: add `isTelegramRichMessageInvalidError` and catch `RICH_MESSAGE_*_INVALID` errors in `sendChunkedText`, falling back to plain-text `sendMessage` delivery instead of silently dropping the outbound message. - `extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts`: update `mailto:` expectations; add regression coverage for auto-detected emails staying plain text in rich HTML. - `extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts`: add regression tests that `RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID` and `RICH_MESSAGE_URL_INVALID` trigger plain-text fallback. ## Real behavior proof - **Behavior addressed**: Telegram rich-message delivery rejects OAuth/status text containing email-like auth profile labels when `richMessages=true`; after the fix, rich HTML no longer emits `mailto:` anchors and oversized/invalid rich payloads fall back to plain text on the outbound send path. - **Real environment tested**: live Telegram Bot API 10+ against bot `@LocalViewShen_bot`, DM chat id `7985450746`, plus local formatter verification and unit tests on branch `fix/telegram-rich-message-email-invalid`. - **Exact steps**: (1) send a provider-prefixed `mailto:` rich HTML payload that reproduces `RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID`; (2) generate `/status`-shaped text through patched `markdownToTelegramRichHtml` and send via `sendRichMessage`; (3) send OAuth-shaped status text (`openai:user@example.com`) through the patched formatter and live API; (4) run formatter spot checks and `format.test.ts` / `send.test.ts`. - **Evidence after fix**: (1) broken provider-prefixed `mailto:` payload returns `400 Bad Request: RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID`; (2) patched formatter output `🔑 oauth (default (user@example.com))` has `Has mailto: false` and live `sendRichMessage` returns `ok: true` (`message_id: 135`); (3) OAuth-shaped label sends successfully (`message_id: 137`); probe messages deleted after verification; (4) unit tests pass (195 total). - **Observed result**: invalid provider-prefixed `mailto:` rich HTML still fails at the API as expected; fixed rich HTML delivers successfully with email text visible and no `mailto:` anchor; HTTPS links remain clickable. - **What was not tested**: Mantis Telegram Desktop screen recording; end-to-end `/status` through a running gateway with real auth-profile labels; shared reply-delivery fallback in `bot/delivery.send.ts` (Layer 1 prevention already affects shared rich HTML; Layer 2 fallback currently covers `sendMessageTelegram` only). ## Evidence **Live Telegram Bot API (repro helper):** ```bash export TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN="" export TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID="7985450746" export TELEGRAM_PROXY="socks5h://127.0.0.1:7898" # only if api.telegram.org is not directly reachable ./scripts/local-telegram-rich-proof.sh ``` **Baseline (broken rich HTML — reproduces failure):** ```bash curl -sS --proxy "${TELEGRAM_PROXY:-}" \ "https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/sendRichMessage" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"chat_id":"7985450746","rich_message":{"html":"openai:user@example.com"}}' ``` Observed: ```json { "ok": false, "error_code": 400, "description": "Bad Request: RICH_MESSAGE_EMAIL_INVALID" } ``` **After fix (patched formatter + live send):** ```bash FIXED_HTML="$(node --import tsx --no-warnings -e " import { markdownToTelegramRichHtml } from './extensions/telegram/src/format.ts'; process.stdout.write(markdownToTelegramRichHtml('🔑 oauth (default (user@example.com))')); ")" curl -sS --proxy "${TELEGRAM_PROXY:-}" \ "https://api.telegram.org/bot${TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN}/sendRichMessage" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d "{\"chat_id\":\"7985450746\",\"rich_message\":{\"html\":\"${FIXED_HTML}\"}}" ``` Observed: ``` Generated HTML: 🔑 oauth (default (user@example.com)) Has mailto: false API: ok=true, message_id=135, chat_id=7985450746 deleteMessage: ok=true ``` **OAuth-shaped status label:** ``` Input: 🔑 openai:user@example.com (user@example.com) HTML: 🔑 openai:user@example.com (user@example.com) Live sendRichMessage: ok=true, message_id=137 (probe deleted) ``` **Formatter spot checks:** ``` Test 1: 🔑 oauth (default (user@example.com)) → no mailto ✓ Test 2: [support](mailto:help@company.com) → plain "support" ✓ Test 3: [docs](https://example.com/docs) → HTTPS preserved ✓ Test 4: /status snippet with email + HTTPS link → mailto absent ✓ ``` **Unit tests:** ```bash pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts ``` ``` ✓ extensions/telegram/src/format.test.ts — 59 passed ✓ extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts — 136 passed Total — 195 passed ```
target.author: hugenshen

target.title: fix(cron): pass allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent from cron executor for both runner paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224: cron jobs that intentionally produce empty replies (silent alert watchers — "watch X, say nothing unless there is something to report") fail every run with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` even when the underlying runner returns success.

Supersedes PR #94491 which used a lane substring heuristic in the shared CLI runner. Per ClawSweeper review, the correct boundary is the cron executor.

## Root cause

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for DM/group chat contexts. The cron executor never sets it, so both the CLI and embedded runner paths throw on empty output.

## Changes

In `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`: set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` on both runner calls:

1. **CLI path** (`runCliAgent` call): added flag
2. **Embedded path** (`runEmbeddedAgent` call): added flag

```typescript
// Intentionally-silent cron jobs (watchers that say nothing unless
// there is something to report) should not throw empty_response.
allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
```

This is a +6 line change to a single file.

## Why this is better than PR #94491

| PR #94491 (old) | This PR (new) |
|----------------|---------------|
| Lane substring check in shared `cli-runner.ts` | Flag set at cron executor boundary |
| Only covers CLI runner path | Covers CLI + embedded runner paths |
| Broadens shared guard for any lane containing "cron" | No shared guard change |
| `!String(lane).includes("cron")` heuristic | Explicit `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Cron jobs with intentionally empty replies fail with `empty_response` error on both CLI and embedded runner paths.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full repository checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```shell
  $ node /tmp/proof-94491-v2.mjs
  ```
  The script verifies the flag propagation for both CLI and embedded paths, DM/chat non-regression, message tool delivery, and text output delivery.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output:
  ```
  ======================================================================
  PR #94491 v2 Live Proof — cron executor allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent
  ======================================================================

  Case 1: CLI runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 2: Embedded runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 3: DM chat (no flag) — empty reply still throws
    result: FailoverError: empty_response
    status: PASS (correctly throws)

  Case 4: Cron job with actual text — normal delivery
    result: All systems nominal.
    status: PASS

  Case 5: Cron job using message tool — delivery handled by tool
    result: ok (message tool handled delivery)
    status: PASS

  Case 6: Verification — executor passes flag to BOTH paths
    CLI path has flag: YES
    Embedded path has flag: YES
    Both paths covered: PASS

  ======================================================================
  RESULTS: 6/6 scenarios pass
  ======================================================================
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Both CLI and embedded cron paths accept empty replies without throwing. DM/group chat contexts are unchanged (still throw on empty replies unless their own `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is set).
- **What was not tested**: Live cron job running on a scheduled Gateway (requires Gateway with cron configuration)

## Related

- PR #94491: original lane-heuristic approach (superseded)
- PR #94235: competing executor implementation for same issue

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(slack): preserve custom identity on message edits
target.body:
fix(slack): preserve custom identity on message edits

## Summary

- **Problem:** Slack `chat.update` silently drops `username`/`icon_url`/`icon_emoji` parameters when editing messages. Custom agent display names and avatars revert to the bot's default after any edit (tool calls, streaming updates, partial responses).
- **Fix:** Thread `SlackSendIdentity` through `editSlackMessage` and all its callers, mirroring the same mutually-exclusive `icon_url`/`icon_emoji` branching logic from the send path.
- **Out of scope:** Direct `chat.update` callers in approval-handler, interactions, and assistant modules (these may not use custom identity). Discord and Telegram don't need this fix (identity is webhook-level or bot-level, not per-message).

## Linked context

Closes #58737

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent display name and avatar revert to bot default when Slack messages are edited via `chat.update`. Initial `chat.postMessage` correctly uses custom identity, but edits drop it.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 10, Node.js v22.22.3, OpenClaw latest main, local checkout with the patch applied.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts` — 63/63 passed
  2. Ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/draft-stream.test.ts` — 11/11 passed
  3. Verified `editSlackMessage` now accepts `identity` parameter and builds correct payload
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  63 passed (63)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/draft-stream.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  11 passed (11)
```
- **Observed result after fix:** `editSlackMessage` now accepts an optional `identity` parameter and includes `username`/`icon_url`/`icon_emoji` in the `chat.update` payload. Draft stream edits now pass `identity: params.identity` to preserve custom agent identity during streaming updates. Preview finalize and dispatch now forward `slackIdentity` to edit calls.
- **What was not tested:** Live Slack workspace with custom agent identity (requires Slack bot setup). The test verifies the function signature and payload construction, not actual Slack API behavior.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/action-runtime.test.ts` — 63/63 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/draft-stream.test.ts` — 11/11 passed
- No CHANGELOG.md edits

## Risk checklist

- userVisible: Yes (agent identity preserved on message edits)
- configEnvMigration: No
- securityAuthNetwork: No
- Mitigation: Identity payload is only included when explicitly provided. Follows same pattern as send path. Graceful degradation if `chat:write.customize` scope is missing.

## Current review state

- Ready for review

target.title: Fix Feishu card action mention gating
target.body:
Fixes #85450.

## Summary

- Mark Feishu synthetic group card-action callbacks with bot mention metadata when `botOpenId` is known.
- Keep p2p card callbacks unchanged so direct-message callbacks do not gain synthetic mentions.
- Add regression coverage for both the group mention metadata and the p2p no-mention behavior.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu structured card button callbacks in `requireMention: true` group chats were synthesized as group text messages without bot mention metadata, so the existing group activation gate could treat the callback as not addressing the bot and drop it. After this patch, synthetic group card callbacks include a bot mention entry when `botOpenId` is available, while p2p callbacks still omit synthetic mentions.

Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/googlerest/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src`, branch `codex/fix-85450-feishu-card-mention`, commit `2482f7f442 Fix Feishu card action mention gating`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.card-action.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/policy.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local OpenClaw checkout:

```text
RUN  v4.1.6 /Users/googlerest/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-src

Test Files  3 passed (3)
     Tests  102 passed (102)
  Start at  02:24:43
  Duration  3.34s (transform 1.01s, setup 33ms, import 2.34s, tests 843ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: The Feishu card-action regression now asserts that a synthetic group callback has `message.chat_type === "group"` and includes `mentions: [{ key: "mention_bot", id: { open_id: "ou_bot" }, name: "bot" }]`. The same suite also asserts that p2p card callbacks keep `message.mentions` undefined. The broader Feishu bot and policy regression files passed with the same patch. `git diff --check` completed with no output.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Feishu group chat card click because this local environment does not have live Feishu credentials.

target.title: fix(zalouser): bound non-interactive media sends
target.body:
Closes #91524

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users sending media or files through the zalouser channel could have non-interactive sends hang indefinitely when the underlying Zalo media upload callback never arrived.

## Why This Change Was Made

zalouser now bounds the zca-js upload-callback wait used by non-interactive outbound media delivery and returns an actionable failure instead of leaving CLI, agent-message, or cron flows stuck on an unsettled promise. Text-only sends, untimed media sends, and GIF media stay on the existing zca-js send paths. The fix does not add a new user config or environment surface.

## User Impact

Automated zalouser media/file sends no longer hang forever in the known callback-loss case. If the live upload callback path is unavailable, users get a bounded error that points them back to a connected gateway listener session instead of an eventual Node top-level-await exit.

## Evidence

- Focused regression: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.vitest-cache-91524 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.credentials.test.ts` -> 13 passed.
- Formatting: `node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/zalouser/src/types.ts extensions/zalouser/src/channel.adapters.ts extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.ts extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.credentials.test.ts extensions/zalouser/src/zca-client.ts` -> passed.
- Whitespace: `git diff --check` -> passed.
- Production typecheck: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p extensions/zalouser/tsconfig.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/zalouser-91524.tsbuildinfo` -> exit 0.
- Extension test typecheck: `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-91524.tsbuildinfo` -> exit 0.
- Lint: `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/zalouser/src/types.ts extensions/zalouser/src/channel.adapters.ts extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.ts extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.credentials.test.ts extensions/zalouser/src/zca-client.ts` -> exit 0.
- Autoreview: `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main --prompt ...` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings.

Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: zalouser media/file sends with the non-interactive channel timeout now return a bounded failure while waiting for zca-js upload callbacks, before final media delivery is attempted.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout with `zca-js@2.1.2` package source inspected for `uploadAttachment`, `sendMessage`, and `custom` API behavior, plus regression tests simulating unresolved upload callbacks and the uploaded-attachment final send path.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.vitest-cache-91524 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/zalouser/src/zalo-js.credentials.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: the regression tests create saved zalouser sessions, mock outbound media loading for PDF/GIF media, simulate an unresolved `api.uploadAttachment`, and verify `sendZaloTextMessage` returns `ok: false` with the timeout message before calling final send. They also verify untimed media stays on zca-js `sendMessage`, GIF media stays on the zca-js GIF-capable path, and timed non-GIF media sends from the already uploaded attachment without re-entering zca-js `sendMessage` with attachments.

Observed result after fix: Vitest reported `Test Files 1 passed` and `Tests 13 passed`; the bounded-hang, no-timeout, uploaded-attachment, and GIF regression tests passed.

What was not tested: live Zalo Personal account delivery was not exercised in this environment, so this PR proves bounded failure for the callback-loss path and preserves tested send-path routing rather than proving successful live platform upload receipt.

target.title: fix(secrets): strip control characters from secret input
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`normalizeSecretInput()` strips line breaks and non-Latin1 characters from copied credentials, but it still preserved other embedded terminal/header control characters such as NUL, TAB, DEL, and C1 controls. Those characters can still make downstream header construction fail or carry invisible garbage in configured secrets.

## Why This Change Was Made

The existing character filter now also drops C0/C1/DEL control characters while continuing to preserve printable Latin-1 and ordinary internal spaces. This keeps the documented `Bearer <token>` style behavior intact.

A focused regression test covers embedded controls and verifies ordinary spaces are still preserved.

## User Impact

Copied API keys and tokens with hidden control characters normalize to usable values instead of failing later in provider request setup. Human-readable values with intentional spaces remain unchanged apart from edge trimming.

## Evidence

Direct behavior probe:


target.title: fix(gateway): expose image generation provider inventory over RPC
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?
- Console needs to periodically spawn CLI process (`openclaw infer image providers --json`) to render image generation provider settings, causing CPU, memory, disk I/O pressure on small hosted machines

Why does this matter now?
- Console needs to display image generation provider settings page, currently requires spawning CLI process with high overhead

What is the intended outcome?
- Add read-only `image.providers` Gateway RPC exposing provider metadata (id, label, configured, defaultModel, models, capabilities) without credentials

What is intentionally out of scope?
- No new provider configuration functionality, only exposes existing metadata
- No changes to authentication flow or config storage

What does success look like?
- Gateway RPC `image.providers` returns complete metadata for 13 image generation providers
- Follows the same pattern as `tts.providers`

What should reviewers focus on?
- Verify use of canonical `loadAuthProfileStoreForRuntime` instead of hardcoded env vars
- Verify active provider resolution logic (no fallback to first provider)

---

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?
- Closes #78330

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?
- Related `tts.providers` implementation as pattern reference

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
- Issue author: chenyanchen (Console team)

---

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Add `image.providers` Gateway RPC endpoint exposing image generation provider metadata

- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw environment, Gateway running on localhost:18789

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
# Gateway RPC call
pnpm openclaw gateway call image.providers
# Or direct curl
curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18789/rpc \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \
  -d '{"method":"image.providers","params":{}}'
```

- **Evidence after fix**:
```json

    {
      "id": "comfy",

        "geometry": false,
        "output": []
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "openai",
      "label": "OpenAI",
      "configured": false,
      "defaultModel": "gpt-image-2",
      "models": ["gpt-image-2", "gpt-image-1.5", "gpt-image-1", "gpt-image-1-mini"],
      "capabilities": {
        "generate": { "maxCount": 4, "supportsSize": true },
        "edit": { "enabled": true, "maxCount": 4, "maxInputImages": 5 },
        "geometry": { "sizes": ["1024x1024", "1536x1024", "1024x1536", "2048x2048", "2048x1152", "3840x2160", "2160x3840"] },
        "output": { "formats": ["png", "jpeg", "webp"], "qualities": ["low", "medium", "high", "auto"], "backgrounds": ["transparent", "opaque", "auto"] }
      }
    },
    // ... 13 providers total
  ],
  "active": null
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Successfully returned complete metadata for 13 image generation providers
  - Response format passes schema validation
  - No credentials or secrets included

- **What was tested**:
  - Gateway RPC call
  - CLI call (`pnpm openclaw infer image providers --json`)
  - 20 unit tests
  - Schema validation

- **What was not tested**:
  - Integration test in real Console UI

---

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?
```bash
# Build
pnpm build

# Tests
pnpm test src/gateway/server-methods/image.test.ts

# Gateway RPC
pnpm openclaw gateway call image.providers

# CLI comparison

```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Schema validation tests

What failed before this fix, if known?

If no test was added, why not?

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (No)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (No)

How is that risk mitigated?

target.title: feat: add unified Amazon AWS services plugin (Polly TTS, Transcribe STT, Nova Sonic voice)
target.body:
## Summary

Add a bundled Amazon plugin providing three AWS AI services:
- **Amazon Polly**: text-to-speech (generative/neural/standard engines, 16 voices)
- **Amazon Transcribe**: speech-to-text (streaming transcription, auto-format conversion)
- **Amazon Nova Sonic**: realtime bidirectional voice conversations (full-duplex with tool calling)

All services authenticate via the **standard AWS IAM credential chain** (instance roles, SSO, access keys). No separate API key needed.

Supersedes #64318 (closed due to stale branch after repo restructure). All maintainer feedback from @steipete has been addressed.

## Changes

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `extensions/amazon/index.ts` | Plugin entry — registers all three providers with null-checks for disabled state |
| `extensions/amazon/polly/speech-provider.ts` | Polly TTS provider with voice directives, engine selection, opus conversion |
| `extensions/amazon/polly/tts.ts` | Polly SDK wrapper with retry logic |
| `extensions/amazon/transcribe/media-understanding-provider.ts` | Transcribe STT provider |
| `extensions/amazon/transcribe/stt.ts` | Transcribe Streaming SDK wrapper with format conversion + retry |
| `extensions/amazon/nova-sonic/bridge.ts` | Nova Sonic bidirectional stream bridge (correct AWS event protocol) |
| `extensions/amazon/nova-sonic/realtime-voice-provider.ts` | Nova Sonic provider registration |
| `extensions/amazon/nova-sonic/bridge.test.ts` | Protocol shape tests (6 cases) |
| `extensions/amazon/polly/speech-provider.test.ts` | Polly unit tests |
| `extensions/amazon/transcribe/media-understanding-provider.test.ts` | Transcribe unit tests |
| `extensions/amazon/nova-sonic/realtime-voice-provider.test.ts` | Nova Sonic provider tests |
| `docs/providers/amazon.md` | Provider documentation |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | Changelog entry |
| `.github/labeler.yml` | Label config for `extensions/amazon/**` |

## Key design decisions

1. **Nova Sonic protocol**: Implements the full AWS bidirectional event sequence per [AWS docs](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/nova/latest/nova2-userguide/sonic-input-events.html): `sessionStart` (with `turnDetectionConfiguration`) → `promptStart` (voice, tools, audio config) → system prompt as `contentStart/textInput/contentEnd` → audio `contentStart` with `audioInputConfiguration` → streaming `audioInput` → close sequence.

2. **Disabled services**: Each service independently disableable via `config.{service}.enabled: false`. Returns `null` from builder, caller skips registration. Consistent pattern across all three.

3. **Retry logic**: Exponential backoff (3 attempts, 500ms/1s/2s) for transient AWS errors (ThrottlingException, ServiceUnavailable) in Polly and Transcribe.

4. **UUID generation**: Uses `crypto.randomUUID()` for Nova Sonic `promptName`/`contentName` identifiers.

## Real behavior proof

**Environment:** EC2 instance (`arm64`, `us-east-1`) with IAM instance role, Node v22.22.0.

### Polly TTS ✅

```
Polly SUCCESS
  Voice: Ruth (generative engine)
  Audio bytes returned: 31292
  ContentType: audio/mpeg
  RequestId: cbb7e670-9832-4271-bb4e-3f6c1acf97fc
```

### Transcribe STT ✅

```
Transcribe SUCCESS — stream established
  RequestId: af5e5475-9354-4aa8-9fc5-02550c60c45d
  SessionId: 23825233-a05a-4808-8f09-c2dd4a3ba82e
  LanguageCode: en-US
  Events received: 3
```

### Nova Sonic (Bedrock bidirectional stream) ✅

```
Nova Sonic SDK setup SUCCESS — client ready for bidirectional stream
  Region: us-east-1, Model: amazon.nova-sonic-v1:0
  Auth: IAM instance role (SigV4), credentials resolved

Stream result: RequestId=f13714fd-c095-471f-96c9-e56350f3d93a
  "No prompts were received in the sequence"
  (Auth succeeded — error is protocol-level: test sent sessionStart + sessionEnd
   without promptStart, confirming the full event sequence in the PR is required)
```

### What was not tested

- Full Nova Sonic voice conversation (requires sustained audio I/O)
- Live OpenClaw gateway integration (plugin registration → user-facing TTS/STT)
- Voice-note Opus conversion (requires ffmpeg in test env)

## Compatibility

- Backward compatible: Yes (new extension, no existing behavior changed)
- Config/env changes: New `plugins.entries.amazon.config` section (optional)
- Migration needed: No

target.title: fix: route durable reasoning block/final payloads to channels with dedicated reasoning lanes
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #94937 — `/reasoning on` drops the thinking lane on Telegram.

Two layers of defense were dropping `isReasoning` payloads before Telegram could render them:

1. Shared dispatch (`dispatch-from-config.ts`) — unconditionally suppressed `isReasoning`
2. Outbound normalization (`createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry`) — called by Telegram's `normalizeDeliveryPayload`, dropped any payload where `shouldSuppressReasoningPayload` returned true

### Changes

**`src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts`** — Narrow reasoning bypass to Telegram only (was 4 channels). Discord was fixed in #95029 (merged).

**`extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts`** — Strip `isReasoning` before outbound normalization so the second layer doesn't drop it. Original marker still consumed by lane splitting.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: `/reasoning on` delivers durable thinking messages on Telegram instead of silently dropping them.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x86_64. Full data path traced from agent payload through both suppression layers to Telegram lane splitting.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```
$ git diff HEAD~2 --stat
 2 files changed, 18 insertions(+), 9 deletions(-)

$ echo "=== Fix 1: dispatch-from-config.ts — Telegram-only ==="
$ grep "channel !== \"telegram\"" src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.ts
                  channel !== "telegram"

$ echo "=== Fix 2: bot-message-dispatch.ts — strip isReasoning before normalization ==="
$ grep -A5 "Strip isReasoning" extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.ts
                    // Strip isReasoning before outbound normalization so the
                    // shared createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry path (which calls
                    // shouldSuppressReasoningPayload) does not drop the payload.
                    const preNormalizedPayload =
                      payload.isReasoning === true
                        ? { ...payload, isReasoning: false }
                        : payload;

$ echo "=== Second drop point: createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry ==="
$ grep -A2 "shouldSuppressReasoningPayload" src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts
  if (shouldSuppressReasoningPayload(payload)) {
    return null;
  }

$ echo "=== Discord fixed separately ==="
$ gh pr view 95029 --repo openclaw/openclaw --json state,mergedAt | python3 -m json.tool
#95029 merged, no longer blocking
```

**After-fix evidence**: Two guards now protect Telegram reasoning:
1. `dispatch-from-config.ts` — block reply (line ~3108) and final reply loop (line ~3284) pass `isReasoning` for `telegram`
2. `bot-message-dispatch.ts` — strips `isReasoning` before `normalizeDeliveryPayload` so `createOutboundPayloadPlanEntry` does not call `shouldSuppressReasoningPayload` on it

**Observed result after the fix**: Telegram reasoning payloads survive both layers and reach lane splitting where `payload.isReasoning` routes text into `lanes.reasoning` for persistent Thinking display.

**What was not tested**: Live Telegram session with reasoning model. Both drop points confirmed by source analysis — `shouldSuppressReasoningPayload` and the 4-channel allowlist both unconditionally deleted before the payload reached lane splitting.

target.title: feat(cli): openclaw attach — launch an external harness bound to a gateway session
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

PR #96351 added the gateway-side **attach grant** primitive — per-session, scoped, revocable MCP loopback access — but there is no user-facing way to use it. A user who wants Claude Code or OpenCode to reach the gateway's scoped tools would have to hand-mint a grant, hand-write the harness's MCP config with the right headers, set the token env, and remember to revoke on exit.

## Why This Change Was Made

This is the launcher half of the `attach` surface (tracking: openclaw/openclaw#96205). `openclaw attach` mints a per-session grant via `attach.grant`, realizes the returned loopback MCP config into the harness's own config format, spawns the harness interactively, and revokes the grant on exit. The token is passed via env, never argv or a durable file. The grant — not a process-global token — is what keeps this a lower-trust, revocable boundary.

**Stacked on #96351** (the gateway primitive). Until #96351 merges, this PR's diff includes its gateway commit; the reviewable PR2 surface is the four CLI files (`src/cli/attach-cli.ts` + its two tests + two registration lines in `register.subclis-core.ts` / `subcli-descriptors.ts`).

## User Impact

New `openclaw attach [--harness claude|opencode] [--session <key>] [--ttl <ms>] [--bin <path>] [--print-config]`.
- **Additive** — a new CLI command; no change to existing commands.
- **Claude Code**: `--mcp-config` with the gateway config verbatim. **OpenCode**: `opencode.json` remote-MCP entry + `OPENCODE_CONFIG`.
- **`--print-config`**: setup mode — mints the grant, writes the config, prints the launch command, and leaves the grant live + the file in place so you can launch the harness yourself.
- **No new gateway config/env surface** (reuses PR1's `OPENCLAW_MCP_*` placeholders).

## Evidence

- **Unit tests (11)**: adapters (config realization for both harnesses + bin/filenames/`$schema`) and the command action via a mocked gateway boundary — mint→realize→`--print-config` (no revoke), the harness/`__proto__`/`--ttl` guards, and malformed-response handling.
- **`openclaw attach --help`** renders the command + options (real CLI).
- The gateway side (`attach.grant`/`attach.revoke`) is proven live in #96351.
- **Gates** (SHA `a2b6c247752`, stacked on #96351; run in an isolated worktree): build ✓ · oxlint 0 · 11 tests · tsgo 0 · autoreview (findings fixed/rejected) · clawsweeper (grant-response validation added; SIGINT/gateway-TTL/`0o600`/spawn-sync findings triaged) · mutation **66.7%** (86/129) — surviving mutants are cosmetic CLI strings (option descriptions, help text, log messages, temp-path prefixes), not logic; brittle string assertions were rejected per AGENTS.md ("prefer behavior tests over string greps").

**Risks → mitigations:** leaked grant → TTL + revoke-on-exit; malformed gateway response → validated at the RPC boundary (fails loudly, no crash); double-SIGINT → no manual signal forwarding (the child shares the TTY foreground group); `__proto__` harness → `Object.hasOwn` guard.

target.title: feat(mcp): add shared bundled runtime scope
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #95506. Bundled MCP runtimes are session-scoped today, so matching sessions in the same workspace/config can repeatedly start the same MCP server process set and rebuild the same tool catalog. That is useful isolation by default, but it is expensive for deployments that intentionally share MCP state across sessions.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an opt-in `mcp.runtimeScope` config with the default preserved as `session`. When set to `shared`, the runtime manager keys a shared runtime by workspace directory plus the loaded MCP config fingerprint, attaches multiple session IDs to that runtime, reuses in-flight creation, and only disposes the runtime when the last attached session is gone or idle eviction applies. The shared runtime key is synthetic and hashed, so the underlying runtime is not accidentally tied to any one public session ID.

## User Impact

Existing configurations keep per-session isolation. Users who opt into `mcp.runtimeScope: "shared"` can reuse one bundled MCP runtime/catalog/process set across matching sessions, reducing MCP startup churn while still separating different workspaces or different MCP configs.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` passed: 40 tests, including real stdio MCP proof that default session scope starts two server processes/list calls for two sessions while shared scope reuses one runtime/server process/list call.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/config/doc-baseline.test.ts src/config/doc-baseline.integration.test.ts` passed: 2 shards, 31 tooling tests + 62 runtime-config tests.
- `corepack pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- 2026-06-24 maintenance rebase on `af2b0a611`: re-ran `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` (44 tests), `node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check`, `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`, and `git diff --check`; all passed.
- 2026-06-24 redacted real OpenClaw bundled MCP runtime proof: `node --import tsx` invoked the production `getOrCreateSessionMcpRuntime` path with a real stdio MCP child process and two session IDs against the same workspace/config. Redacted terminal output:

```text
OpenClaw bundled MCP runtime proof
SCENARIO session
  runtime-object-reused=false
  runtime-a-session-id=session-session-a
  runtime-b-session-id=session-session-b
  catalog-a-tools=proofProbe/session_proof_tool
  catalog-b-tools=proofProbe/session_proof_tool
  server-start-count=2
  initialize-count=2
  tools-list-count=2
```

target.title: fix(tools): propagate external content provenance to policy hooks
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

External content is already wrapped as untrusted prompt text by `wrapExternalContent`. Before this change, the post-model tool-call policy boundary had no structured signal showing whether untrusted external content was present in the model context that led to a tool call.

That left policy plugins with tool name, params, run ID, derived paths, and related context, but no structured way to tell whether a call followed content from `web_fetch`, browser output, email, webhook input, API input, channel metadata, or web search.

This PR also fixes several correctness issues found while proving that flow:

- The detector only recognized synthetic marker shapes, not production `<<<EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="...">>>` markers.
- Prompt provenance could be seeded into a copied catalog context that normal core tools did not receive before their first hook call.
- `catalogToolHookContext` was used before declaration in the embedded runner path.
- Provenance scanning could touch hostile result objects with throwing getters or proxy traps.

## What Changed

- Adds `detectToolHookExternalContentProvenance` in `src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts`.
- Parses production external-content markers emitted by `wrapExternalContent`.
- Adds public plugin hook types:
  - `PluginHookExternalContentSource`
  - `PluginHookExternalContentProvenance`
- Adds optional `externalContent` to:
  - `HookContext`
  - `PluginHookToolContext`
  - `PluginHookBeforeToolCallEvent`
  - trusted tool policy events
- Propagates `externalContent` through `before_tool_call` hook events and trusted tool policy evaluation.
- Refreshes same-run provenance after wrapped tool results.
- Tracks same-run provenance by `runId` with a bounded cache.
- Updates `createOpenClawCodingTools` so the shared `beforeToolCallHookContext` object is mutated in place instead of copied.
- Moves `catalogToolHookContext` above the `toolsRaw` construction path that needs it.
- Restricts `Source:` label parsing to the marker metadata section before the `---` separator, so source labels inside untrusted body text are ignored.
- Guards result scanning and provenance refresh so throwing getters or proxy traps cannot fail a successful tool call.
- Documents the new plugin hook field and best-effort semantics.
- Updates the plugin SDK surface budget for the new public hook contract.

## Rebase and Conflict Status

Rebased onto current `main` with one commit and the same intended 8 changed files. GitHub now reports the PR as mergeable.

Latest pushed head:

```text
head: 735ec7b20eac8892d44aaa336b6aa67d97fa8925
base: main
changed files: 8
additions: 766
deletions: 40
```

The only conflict was in `scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs`. The resolution keeps current-main budget values and applies only this PR's public SDK budget delta:

```js
publicEntrypoints: 322        // unchanged from current main
publicExports: 10381          // +4 for new non-deprecated hook types/fields
publicFunctionExports: 5206   // unchanged from current main
publicDeprecatedExports: 3247 // unchanged from current main (PR adds no deprecated exports)
```

Post-rebase diff shape:

```text
docs/plugins/hooks.md
scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs
src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts
src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.ts
src/agents/agent-tools.ts
src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts
src/plugin-sdk/types.ts
src/plugins/hook-types.ts
```

## Compatibility and Security Notes

This adds a public plugin SDK contract. The source taxonomy and field semantics should be reviewed as public API surface before merge.

`externalContent` is a host-derived, best-effort provenance signal. It means wrapped external content was observed in model context or same-run tool output context. It is not a complete taint-tracking proof, and policy plugins should not treat it as a full security attestation.

The signal is monotonic within a run. Once an external source is observed for a run, later tool policy hooks in that run receive the merged source set.

## Evidence

- Real environment tested: Local Linux source checkout, branch `fix/tool-call-external-provenance`, head `735ec7b20eac8892d44aaa336b6aa67d97fa8925`, Node 26.0.0, pnpm 11.2.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Rebased the PR onto current `upstream/main` in a clean worktree.
  2. Resolved the only conflict in `scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` by keeping current-main budget values and applying only this PR's public SDK export delta (`publicExports: 10381`).
  3. Ran the full external-content provenance e2e suite against the rebased head.
  4. Ran the plugin SDK surface budget check against the rebased head.
  5. Ran focused oxlint checks for the changed source/type/script files.
  6. Ran test type checks (core + extensions).
- Evidence after fix:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts \
  src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts
```

```text
  ✓ ...passes external content provenance to trusted tool policies
  ✓ ...refreshes external content provenance after a same-run external tool result
  ✓ ...seeds external content provenance from prompt history before exec hooks
  ✓ ...shares same-run external content provenance across split core tool wrapper contexts
  ✓ ...ignores spoofed source labels inside untrusted external content body

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       64 passed (64)
```

- Observed result after fix:
  - The rebased head has no merge conflicts and GitHub reports the PR as mergeable.
  - The e2e suite exercises the changed before_tool_call provenance path, including production marker detection, trusted policy propagation, same-run tool-result provenance refresh, prompt-history seeding, split core-tool wrapper contexts, spoofed source labels, and guarded traversal for hostile result objects.
  - The SDK surface check confirms the public package export budget for the new hook contract. The deprecated-export budget is kept at current-main value (3247) since this PR adds no deprecated exports.
  - Lint and test type checks pass.
- What was not tested:
  - No production provider credentials, channel messages, or live web requests were used.
  - No live deployed OpenClaw instance was exercised.

target.title: fix(security): add ReDoS guard to exec approval argPattern matching
target.body:
Fixes #90014

## Problem

`matchArgPattern` in `src/infra/exec-command-resolution.ts` (line 323) compiles user-provided `argPattern` strings via `new RegExp(argPattern)` without any ReDoS validation. While patterns auto-generated by `buildArgPatternFromArgv` are safe (they use literal escaping), legacy hand-authored patterns from the exec-approvals allowlist could contain nested repetition (e.g., `(a+)+$`) that causes catastrophic backtracking.

## Fix

Add a `hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)` guard before `new RegExp(argPattern)`. If the pattern contains nested repetition, return `false` (no match) instead of compiling the dangerous pattern.

**Key design choice:** The guard calls `hasNestedRepetition` (validation only) then compiles with `new RegExp(argPattern)` using the **original untrimmed pattern text**. This preserves exact `argPattern` semantics: leading/trailing whitespace in allowlist patterns is not stripped, so existing policy is unchanged. An earlier revision used `compileSafeRegex` which trims `.source` before compiling; that was replaced to avoid altering policy semantics.

The production diff is +4 lines (1 import, 3 guard lines). No CHANGELOG changes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ReDoS-vulnerable `argPattern` entries in exec-approval allowlists could cause catastrophic backtracking. The `hasNestedRepetition` guard rejects unsafe patterns at validation time before they reach `new RegExp`.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 6006a5c) at `/tmp/fix-82950`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
Step 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82950
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

Step 2. Verify hasNestedRepetition guard is in compiled production code
grep -n "hasNestedRepetition" dist/exec-safe-bin-trust-Cc923KVT.js

Step 3. Exercise the guard directly from compiled production code
node --input-type=module -e "
import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from './dist/safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js';
console.log('Testing ReDoS patterns:');
console.log('  (a+)+\$  =>', hasNestedRepetition('(a+)+\$'));
console.log('  (\\\\d+)+\$ =>', hasNestedRepetition('(\\\\d+)+\$'));
console.log('  ^script\\\\.py\$ =>', hasNestedRepetition('^script\\\\.py\$'));
console.log('  .* =>', hasNestedRepetition('.*'));
"

Step 4. Start gateway to confirm the module loads cleanly
timeout 8 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Compiled production code verification**

The `hasNestedRepetition` guard is present in the compiled exec-approval resolution bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "hasNestedRepetition" dist/exec-safe-bin-trust-Cc923KVT.js
2:import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from "./safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js";
613:if (hasNestedRepetition(argPattern)) return false;
```

**Live ReDoS guard exercised from compiled production code**

Ran the compiled `hasNestedRepetition` function from the production bundle against known ReDoS attack vectors and safe patterns:

```console
$ node --input-type=module -e "import { r as hasNestedRepetition } from './dist/safe-regex-BN8zt3tw.js'; ..."
Testing ReDoS patterns:
  (a+)+$  => true
  (\d+)+$ => true
  ^script\.py$ => false
  .* => false
```

**What this shows:**
- `(a+)+$` (classic ReDoS): **true** (blocked by guard, preventing catastrophic backtracking)
- `(\d+)+$` (digit variant): **true** (blocked)
- `^script\.py$` (safe literal pattern): **false** (allowed, compiles normally)
- `.*` (safe wildcard): **false** (allowed)

Without this guard, `new RegExp("(a+)+$").test("a".repeat(30) + "!")` hangs for 11+ seconds (exponential backtracking). With the guard, the unsafe pattern is rejected in microseconds.

**Live gateway startup proof**

The gateway loads with the patched exec-approval resolution module and starts cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 8 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T11:59:01.406-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T11:59:01.447-07:00 [gateway] resolving authentication…
2026-05-21T11:59:01.456-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T11:59:03.683-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.534-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.535-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 3.1s)
2026-05-21T11:59:04.919-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T11:59:09.985-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T11:59:10.074-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 81ms
```

**Vitest test suite (25 tests, supplemental)**

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts
 ✓ |infra| ../../src/infra/exec-command-resolution.test.ts (25 tests) 37ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  25 passed (25)
```

**RED (guard removed):** Process hangs on catastrophic backtracking and is killed by timeout after 30 seconds. The `(a+)+$` pattern against `"a".repeat(30) + "!"` triggers exponential matching.

Observed result after fix: The compiled `hasNestedRepetition` guard correctly rejects ReDoS-vulnerable patterns like `(a+)+$` and `(\d+)+$` while allowing safe patterns like `^script\.py$`. The guard runs from the production-built dist bundle, the gateway loads cleanly with the patched module, and all 25 tests pass including 10 attack-vector tests.

What was not tested: Live exec approval with a ReDoS-vulnerable argPattern in production config (would require a running agent session with hand-authored exec-approval policies). The guard is exercised through the full `matchAllowlist` integration path and directly from the compiled production bundle.

**Attack vectors tested (via unit tests, supplemental)**
`(a+)+$`, `(\d+)+$`, `([a-z]+)*$`, `(x+x+)+y`, `(a|a?)+$`, `(.*a){25}`, `(\w+)+$`, `(a|aa)+$`

target.title: fix(media): allow SSRF bypass for gateway-localhost URLs (Feishu media fetch)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #82198

When the Feishu channel receives an image, it is saved to the local media store. The agent's image analysis tool then needs to fetch this media, but the URL served by the gateway uses `http://localhost:18888/`, which is blocked by the SSRF security guard (`SsrFBlockedError`).

## Problem

Error log:
```
image failed: Failed to fetch media from http://localhost:18888/: Blocked hostname or private/internal/special-use IP address
```

The SSRF guard in `src/infra/net/ssrf.ts` treats `localhost` as a private/internal address and blocks the fetch. However, this URL is the gateway's own HTTP server — not a remote target — so the SSRF protection should not apply.

## Solution

In `src/media/web-media.ts`, before the `readRemoteMediaBuffer` call that fetches the media via HTTP, detect if the URL points to `localhost` or `127.0.0.1`. If so, add these hostnames to the SSRF policy's `allowedHostnames` list, allowing the gateway to fetch media from its own HTTP server.

This is a targeted exemption: only URLs explicitly constructed by the gateway to reference locally-served media are allowed. The SSRF policy remains intact for all other URLs.

## Changes

`src/media/web-media.ts` — Add `gatewaySsrFPolicy` to allow localhost fetches when the URL points to the gateway's own media server.

## Related

- #82198 (original bug report)
- This also affects other channels (WeChat, WhatsApp) that rely on the same media serving path

target.title: fix(codex): gate CLI session resume behind plugin approval
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `codex.cli.session.resume` did not consistently pass through plugin approval before node dispatch.
- Solution: require a restricted plugin approval for resume, limited to `allow-once` and `deny`.
- What changed: the resume policy now requests approval before `ctx.invokeNode()`, uses the standard plugin approval timeout instead of the longer node resume timeout, fails closed when approval delivery is unavailable or denied, and the Control UI renders only the decisions allowed by the approval request.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): session listing, node-host resume implementation, and Codex CLI spawn behavior are unchanged.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

`codex.cli.session.resume` can resume an existing local Codex CLI session with a caller-supplied prompt. Since this is a dangerous node-host command, the Gateway policy should require an explicit operator decision before forwarding the request to the node.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex CLI session resume now requires plugin approval before node dispatch, and the Control UI shows only the decisions permitted for this approval.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout, branch `codex-resume-approval`, head `713c83eba2ce`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx .artifacts/codex-resume-real-behavior-proof.ts`; `node .artifacts/codex-resume-control-ui-proof.mjs`; targeted Vitest and `oxfmt --check` on touched files.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): copied redacted runtime logs from a live local Gateway + paired node run and from a real Control UI bundle opened in Google Chrome:

```text
[proof ...] proof completed

[control-ui-proof ...] branch=codex-resume-approval head=713c83eba2ce
[control-ui-proof ...] loaded real Control UI bundle from http://127.0.0.1:5173/
[control-ui-proof ...] approval title="Resume Codex CLI session" toolName=codex.cli.session.resume
[control-ui-proof ...] rendered decision buttons=["Allow once","Deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] always allow visible=false
[control-ui-proof ...] click Deny -> handleExecApprovalDecision calls=["deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] rerendered decision buttons=["Allow once","Deny"]
[control-ui-proof ...] click Allow once -> handleExecApprovalDecision calls=["allow-once"]
[control-ui-proof ...] proof completed
```

- Observed result after fix: `codex.cli.sessions.list` still dispatches without plugin approval; `codex.cli.session.resume` creates a plugin approval request before node dispatch; denied approval returns `PLUGIN_APPROVAL_REQUIRED` without dispatch; `allow-once` proceeds to the node handler; the resume approval uses the normal plugin approval timeout rather than the long node resume timeout; the Control UI renders only `Allow once` and `Deny`, with no `Always allow` button.
- What was not tested: remote multi-device deployment and a successful live Codex model turn after resume. The allow path intentionally used an invalid session id so the proof could verify dispatch reaches the node handler without running a model turn.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this change, the Codex resume node-invoke policy dispatched to the node without creating a plugin approval request.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: the Codex CLI resume node-invoke policy did not request plugin approval before dispatching to the node.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no regression test covering approval behavior for this resume policy, and the Control UI approval prompt did not preserve plugin approval `allowedDecisions`.
- Contributing context: session listing and session resume are registered together, but only resume needs approval gating.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/node-cli-sessions.test.ts`, `src/gateway/node-invoke-plugin-policy.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts`, `ui/src/ui/app-gateway.node.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: resume requires plugin approval, does not dispatch when approval delivery is unavailable or denied, does not pass the long node resume timeout into the approval request, forwards only after `allow-once`, and the Control UI renders only the decisions allowed by the plugin approval request.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is local to the Codex node-invoke policy, plugin approval request propagation, and the Control UI approval prompt.
- Existing test that already covers this: none before this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex CLI session resume through this node command now requires plugin approval before dispatch. The approval prompt offers `Allow once` or `Deny`; it does not offer `Always allow` for this resume path.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
resume request -> node dispatch

After:
resume request -> plugin approval (allow-once / deny) -> node dispatch only after allow-once
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes — this narrows the resume execution path by requiring explicit plugin approval before node dispatch.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: command dispatch is narrowed by a fail-closed approval gate.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local development checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel: Codex extension
- Relevant config: temporary local OpenClaw state/config, redacted in logs

### Steps

1. Run the live Gateway + paired node proof.
2. Run the real Control UI decision-rendering proof.
3. Run targeted regression tests and formatting checks.

### Expected

- Resume dispatch only happens after an `allow-once` plugin approval decision.
- Denied approval and unavailable approval runtime do not dispatch to the node.
- The Control UI shows only `Allow once` and `Deny` for the Codex resume approval.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the live Gateway proof, Control UI proof, and targeted regression tests.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: deny blocks dispatch; unavailable approval runtime blocks dispatch; `allow-once` forwards to the node handler; Control UI renders only `Allow once` and `Deny`; Control UI click routing produces `deny` and `allow-once`.
- Edge cases checked: approval runtime unavailable; duplicate/limited plugin approval decisions are normalized before broadcast; disallowed `allow-always` is not rendered by the Control UI.
- What you did **not** verify: remote multi-device deployment and a successful live Codex model turn after resume.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Mostly
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A
- Notes: existing automation that invokes `codex.cli.session.resume` may now need an approval-capable runtime or an explicit approval decision.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: existing automation that resumes Codex CLI sessions may now stop for approval.
  - Mitigation: the change is limited to session resume; session listing and node-host resume behavior remain unchanged after approval.

target.title: fix(qa): record checked-out ref in evidence
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
QA evidence could report a stale environment ref when GITHUB_SHA came from the workflow event or an inherited Crabbox environment instead of the checkout actually under test.

## Why This Change Was Made
Evidence now prefers an explicit OPENCLAW_QA_REF, then resolves the checked-out HEAD from the requested repoRoot, then falls back to GITHUB_SHA for non-git/package environments. Suite, test-file, live transport, and UX Matrix producers pass their repo root through the shared resolver.

## User Impact
QA artifacts identify the source revision that produced them, including manual/ref-selected and remote Crabbox runs. Explicit OPENCLAW_QA_REF behavior remains unchanged.

## Evidence
- Reproduced before fix: checkout ae9474b5fdc0c2f90a2b82356734d86637754d97 reported inherited GITHUB_SHA bd479958c04a1eadbda8b6105e0722588d71e9ad.
- After fix: the same probe reports ae9474b5fdc0c2f90a2b82356734d86637754d97 and preserves an explicit override.
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/evidence-summary.test.ts test/scripts/qa-ux-matrix-evidence-producer.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/telegram/telegram-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/slack/slack-live.runtime.test.ts (77 tests passed)

target.title: fix(gateway): prewarm agent runtime before ready
target.body:
## Summary

- Prewarm the first-turn agent runtime import path during Gateway sidecar startup, before startup-gated Gateway methods are released.
- Keep the prewarm import-only: it does not submit a model request, touch user transcripts, or run tools.
- Add `OPENCLAW_SKIP_STARTUP_AGENT_RUNTIME_PREWARM` as a kill switch and cover the startup ordering/skip behavior in focused Gateway tests.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `git diff --check HEAD~1..HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: After Gateway restart, the first WebChat/agent turn can pay cold in-process agent runtime import/setup cost before trajectory `session.started`, so `chat.send` returns quickly but the visible run appears hung until the runtime path finishes warming.

Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw source checkout on branch `fix/gateway-post-restart-warmup` at `f391104b9597832392329d9e3edd9f8f46010914`, running a foreground dev Gateway on `ws://127.0.0.1:19094` with startup tracing enabled.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```console
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_STARTUP_TRACE=1 pnpm openclaw --dev gateway run --force --port 19094 --auth none --allow-unconfigured --verbose --compact
pnpm openclaw --dev gateway call --url ws://127.0.0.1:19094 --token dev health --json
```

Evidence after fix: Runtime console output from the patched Gateway shows the new import-only prewarm span completed after channels and before plugin services, sidecar readiness, and Gateway ready:

```text
2026-05-16T21:23:03.140-07:00 [gateway/channels] skipping channel start (OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 or OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1)
2026-05-16T21:23:03.144-07:00 [gateway] startup trace: sidecars.channel-skip 4.4ms total=5789.2ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
2026-05-16T21:23:03.153-07:00 [gateway] startup trace: sidecars.channels 8.7ms total=5793.2ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
2026-05-16T21:23:03.200-07:00 [gateway] startup trace: sidecars.agent-runtime-prewarm 40.6ms total=5843.5ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
2026-05-16T21:23:04.687-07:00 [gateway] startup trace: sidecars.plugin-services 1480.2ms total=7331.0ms eventLoopMax=0.0ms
2026-05-16T21:23:04.711-07:00 [gateway] startup trace: sidecars.ready loadedPluginCount=9.0 postReadySidecarCount=0.0
2026-05-16T21:23:04.720-07:00 [gateway] ready
```

Observed result after fix: The same patched Gateway accepted a real Gateway `health` RPC after startup and reported healthy event loop state:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "durationMs": 234,
  "eventLoop": {
    "degraded": false,
    "reasons": [],
    "delayP99Ms": 22.9,
    "delayMaxMs": 117.6
  },
  "plugins": {
    "loaded": [
      "acpx",
      "bonjour",
      "browser",
      "canvas",
      "device-pair",
      "file-transfer",
      "memory-core",
      "phone-control",
      "talk-voice"
    ],
    "errors": []
  }
}
```

What was not tested: A packaged browser WebChat restart E2E was not run from this branch. The runtime proof above uses the foreground source-checkout Gateway and a real Gateway RPC; the patch intentionally does not perform a live model call during startup.

target.title: fix(agent): keep tool media authoritative in replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep tool-produced media authoritative when an assistant reply also contains `MEDIA:` directives, so stale or invented assistant paths cannot override real tool artifacts.
- Preserve attempt-level tool media after pending media is consumed by streamed/final block replies, then use that media when building final run payloads.
- Add browser skill guidance to avoid hand-written `MEDIA:` replies for screenshots.

## Verification

- `pnpm install`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.types.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts src/agents/pi-tool-handler-state.test-helpers.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/tool-media-payloads.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/tool-media-payloads.test.ts src/trajectory/metadata.ts extensions/browser/skills/browser-automation/SKILL.md`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/tool-media-payloads.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/trajectory/metadata.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.types.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.media.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.ts src/agents/pi-tool-handler-state.test-helpers.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/tool-media-payloads.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/tool-media-payloads.test.ts src/trajectory/metadata.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: browser/tool screenshots and other tool media are no longer lost when the assistant reply also includes a stale or invented `MEDIA:` path.

Real environment tested: local Linux OpenClaw source checkout `/tmp/openclaw-media-fix`, foreground gateway from this branch (`pnpm openclaw gateway run --port 18789 --ws-log compact`), OpenClaw `2026.5.17` at commit `7e55485`, bundled browser and telegram plugins loaded, delivered to a Telegram direct chat target.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: stopped the installed user gateway with `openclaw gateway stop --json`; started this branch gateway with `pnpm openclaw gateway run --port 18789 --ws-log compact`; ran `pnpm openclaw agent --agent main --session-id pr-media-proof-bad-media-20260517 --message "PR #82870 conflict proof: open Google News in the browser, take a screenshot, then intentionally include this stale bad media directive in your final reply: MEDIA:/tmp/openclaw-browser-screenshot.png. This verifies tool-media precedence over assistant-authored media. Keep other text short." --deliver --reply-channel telegram --reply-to [redacted-telegram-direct-chat] --json --timeout 600`; checked the outbound media file with `test -s` and `stat`; parsed `/home/zyz/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/pr-media-proof-bad-media-20260517.jsonl` to verify the final assistant text contained the stale path while the browser tool result contained the real screenshot image.

Evidence after fix: runId `8afe4c38-4630-476d-bbd5-fe7af52177cb`; `deliverySucceeded: true`; delivery status `sent`; final assistant raw/visible text was `Opened Google News and captured the screenshot.\n\nMEDIA:/tmp/openclaw-browser-screenshot.png`; final delivered payload used `/home/zyz/.openclaw/media/outbound/56e9f17d-9d98-4786-9ea7-79cde307aba5---7597991d-ae45-4c5f-8c52-569971776fe3.jpg`; that outbound file existed and was `40236 bytes` at `2026-05-17 11:42:47 +0800`; the browser tool screenshot was `/home/zyz/.openclaw/media/browser/56e9f17d-9d98-4786-9ea7-79cde307aba5.png`, `ok: true`, URL `https://news.google.com/home?hl=en-US&gl=US&ceid=US:en`, with an image block present.

Observed result after fix: the Telegram delivery completed with the real screenshot media attached even though the assistant-authored reply included the stale `/tmp/openclaw-browser-screenshot.png` media directive. The stale assistant path did not replace the tool-produced media in the final delivered payload.

What was not tested: full repository CI and non-Telegram delivery channels were not run locally; this live proof used a local Linux foreground gateway and a redacted Telegram direct chat target.

AI-assisted: yes; prepared with Codex.

target.title: docs(telegram): clarify account-local group config
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram docs pointed group setup at root \`channels.telegram.groups\`, which is easy to misread in multi-account configs.
- Why it matters: a bot account can receive a group message from Telegram but still drop it during OpenClaw group policy/auth because the active account has no account-local \`groups\` rule.
- What changed: documented account-local Telegram group rules for multi-account setups and added a non-mutating doctor warning for root groups plus enabled allowlisted accounts missing \`accounts.<id>.groups\`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): runtime authorization and group inheritance behavior are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: A multi-account Telegram setup had a root \`channels.telegram.groups\` rule for a group, but the active bot account lacked \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`. A bot-authored group mention was delivered to the gateway but dropped before agent dispatch.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw gateway with multiple Telegram bot accounts and a private Telegram group used for bot-to-bot coordination. Identifiers are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Verified the local working configuration by copying the group rule into \`channels.telegram.accounts.default.groups["<GROUP_CHAT_ID>"]\`, restarting the installed gateway, and sending a bot-authored \`REQUEST @<bot> ...\` group message from a second trusted bot account.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied redacted runtime log output from the local gateway after the account-local group rule was added:

\`\`\`text
Inbound message telegram:group:<GROUP_CHAT_ID> -> @<BOT_USERNAME> (group, 168 chars)
⇄ res ✓ message.action 310ms channel=telegram conn=<REDACTED> id=<REDACTED>
\`\`\`

- Observed result after fix: Account-local Telegram group config fixed the real setup without a runtime code change. This PR documents that path and adds a doctor warning so similar configs are easier to diagnose.
- What was not tested: I did not deploy this branch as a packaged OpenClaw release. The runtime behavior is unchanged; the new doctor warning is covered by focused tests.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Copied redacted runtime log output from the failing trial before the account-local group rule existed:

\`\`\`text
[telegram][diag] spooled update <UPDATE_ID> completed without agent dispatch kind=message chat=<GROUP_CHAT_ID> chatType=group message=<MESSAGE_ID> from=<TRUSTED_BOT_USER_ID> fromBot=true fromUsername=<TRUSTED_BOT_USERNAME> text="REQUEST @<BOT_USERNAME> <test-message>" outcome=dropped reason=group-policy-or-auth
\`\`\`

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The docs emphasized root \`channels.telegram.groups\` for group setup, while multi-account users need to reason about the active account's group rules.
- Missing detection / guardrail: \`openclaw doctor\` did not flag a config where root Telegram groups existed but enabled allowlisted accounts had no account-local group rules.
- Contributing context (if known): Existing troubleshooting guidance made Telegram privacy/raw delivery and group policy/auth look similar until a diagnostic identified \`group-policy-or-auth\`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: \`extensions/telegram/src/doctor.test.ts\`
- Scenario the test should lock in: root \`channels.telegram.groups\` exists in a multi-account Telegram config, and an enabled allowlisted account has no \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the changed behavior is a non-mutating doctor warning, so the focused doctor scanner/formatter tests cover the new surface directly.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing Telegram doctor tests covered invalid sender IDs, apiRoot warnings, and first-time group guidance, but not this multi-account root-groups trap.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

\`openclaw doctor\` can now warn about multi-account Telegram configs where root group rules exist but enabled allowlisted accounts have no account-local group rules. Telegram docs now show the account-local group config path for multi-account setups.

## Diagram (if applicable)

\`\`\`text
Before:
multi-account Telegram config -> root channels.telegram.groups only -> group update can be dropped by account policy/auth

After:
multi-account Telegram config -> channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups -> account has explicit group authorization
\`\`\`

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any \`Yes\`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw source checkout for tests; installed OpenClaw gateway for real behavior proof
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram
- Relevant config (redacted):

\`\`\`json5
{
  channels: {
    telegram: {
      accounts: {
        default: {
          groups: {
            "<GROUP_CHAT_ID>": {
              requireMention: true,
              allowFrom: ["<OWNER_USER_ID>", "<TRUSTED_BOT_USER_ID>"],
            },
          },
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
\`\`\`

### Steps

1. Configure multiple Telegram bot accounts.
2. Put a group rule only under root \`channels.telegram.groups\`.
3. Send a trusted bot-authored group mention to the active bot account.
4. Move/copy the group rule to \`channels.telegram.accounts.<accountId>.groups\`.
5. Restart the gateway and resend the trusted bot-authored group mention.

### Expected

- Docs make the account-local group path clear for multi-account configs.
- Doctor warns when root groups exist but enabled allowlisted accounts are missing account-local group rules.
- A correctly configured active account receives the trusted group mention as a normal agent turn.

### Actual

- Before the local config fix, the group message was raw-delivered but dropped during group policy/auth.
- After account-local group config, the trusted bot-authored group mention dispatched normally.
- With this PR, focused tests confirm the new doctor warning and skip cases.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local real setup with multi-account Telegram bots; root group rule did not authorize the active account, account-local group rule did.
- Edge cases checked: disabled accounts, \`groupPolicy: "open"\` accounts, mixed open plus allowlisted accounts, single-account configs.
- What you did **not** verify: full packaged release build or live doctor command from an installed package containing this patch.

## Review Conversations

target.title: docs(messages): clarify session key queue lanes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: #72633 was closed because it introduced a broad standalone architecture page without current maintainer ownership.
- Why it matters: the existing Messages page names the routing -> session key -> queue flow, but does not connect session-key routing to queue lanes and the security boundary.
- What changed: add one short clarification under `docs/concepts/messages.md` explaining that `sessionKey` controls the per-session lane, global lane contention, and `session.dmScope` context/queue sharing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no new docs page, no docs nav entry, no glossary/i18n changes, no new terminology surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #72633
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Concrete docs gap

The docs already cover these pieces separately:

- Messages: inbound flow uses `routing/bindings -> session key -> queue -> agent run`.
- Command Queue: embedded runs are serialized by `session:<key>` and then contend for a global lane.
- Session Management: `session.dmScope` changes how direct messages share or isolate session keys.
- Gateway Security: `sessionKey` is routing/context selection, not per-user authorization.

This PR connects those facts in the maintained Messages concept page instead of adding a new architecture guide.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: docs-only clarification of current session-key queue behavior.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on Windows with Node 24.13.0 and GitHub CLI 2.92.0.

```text
$ node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/concepts/messages.md
Docs MDX check passed (1 files, 37ms).

$ node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs docs/concepts/messages.md
checked_internal_links=4374
broken_links=0

$ git diff --check
(no output)
```
- Observed result after fix: `docs/concepts/messages.md` now connects session-key routing, per-session/global queue lanes, DM scope, and the security boundary in one existing concept page.
- What was not tested: full Mintlify publish build; no runtime behavior changed.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

Docs only. No runtime behavior change.

## Diagram (if applicable)

- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
