target.title: feat: add tools.fs.roots — per-agent filesystem roots with access modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `tools.fs.workspaceOnly` is binary, so operators cannot safely allow agents to read or write a specific host path outside the workspace without opening broader filesystem access.
- Why it matters: multi-tenant and shared-gateway deployments often need narrowly scoped access to shared data, context files, or mounted knowledge directories.
- What changed: added `tools.fs.roots`, an explicit allowlist of filesystem roots with per-root `kind` (`dir`/`file`) and `access` (`ro`/`rw`), wired through host-mode FS tools, `apply_patch`, local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery paths, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Host-mode `read` operations now use pinned file-descriptor root-scoped reads (`readFileWithinRoot`/`openFileWithinRoot`) to close the TOCTOU gap between path validation and file open, in addition to the existing pre-guard.
- Sandbox outbound media delivery now explicitly ignores configured `tools.fs.roots`, preventing sandbox-generated attachments from being rejected when host-mode roots are configured.
- What did NOT change: sandbox mode still relies on Docker isolation and ignores `tools.fs.roots`; `exec` sandboxing is unchanged; `tools.allow` / `tools.deny` behavior is unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: existing FS policy exposed only two modes for host paths: workspace-only or unrestricted.
- Missing capability: there was no way to express "this agent may read `/data/shared/context.md` but may not read the rest of the host".
- Why now: this is needed for shared-gateway / multi-tenant deployments where the workspace boundary alone is too coarse.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: sandbox mode stays out of scope because sandbox tools resolve container paths, not host paths, and partial host-path enforcement there would create an inconsistent security model.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should catch this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target tests/files:
  - `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/apply-patch.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `src/media/load-options.test.ts`
  - `src/media/local-media-access.test.ts`
  - `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-media-paths.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/images.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/agents/tools/pdf-tool.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-pinned-write-helper.spawn-error.test.ts`
  - `src/infra/fs-safe.unlink-helper-error.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/actions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/googlechat/src/channel.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts`
  - `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reply-delivery.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - lexical dir/file root matching and ro/rw enforcement
  - canonical-root precedence over alias roots for host-mode mutation paths
  - unlink/delete behavior preserving symlink-entry semantics while still respecting stricter canonical roots
  - roots precedence over `workspaceOnly` for host-mode FS access
  - sandbox-mode fallback still honoring `workspaceOnly`
  - rooted local image/PDF/reference-image loads outside the workspace
  - rooted outbound local-media and reply-delivery paths preserving file-vs-dir root semantics
  - rooted prompt-image auto-load outside the workspace
  - host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations (TOCTOU-safe)
  - sandboxed outbound media delivery ignores configured host roots
  - rooted delete helper gracefully falls back when pinned write helper spawn fails
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky surfaces here are policy resolution, host-path mutation, and media path propagation, so targeted seam tests catch the actual failure modes without requiring a full live deployment.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New config surface: `tools.fs.roots` on global `tools.fs` and per-agent `tools.fs`.
- Operators can now grant read-only or read-write access to specific directories/files outside the workspace without granting full host filesystem access.
- Host-mode `read` / `write` / `edit` / `apply_patch` now respect those explicit roots.
- Local media loading, outbound local-media/reply-delivery flows, and prompt-image auto-load now preserve and enforce rooted file/dir access semantics.
- When both `roots` and `workspaceOnly` are set, `roots` takes precedence for host-mode FS access and a warning is logged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This adds a new operator-controlled path restriction model for host-mode FS access. Risk is policy misunderstanding or incomplete enforcement across FS-adjacent surfaces. Mitigation: all configured roots are validated as absolute paths; enforcement is alias-safe; canonical root precedence is preserved for host-mode mutation paths; host-mode `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load are covered; host-mode reads use pinned-FD root-scoped operations to close TOCTOU gaps; sandbox mode explicitly ignores roots to avoid host/container path confusion.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 / darwin 25.3.0
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A for core FS policy; targeted local media tests use mocked image/PDF/image-generation providers
- Integration/channel (if any): host-mode FS tools + local media loading + outbound local-media reply flows
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json5
{
  "tools": {
    "fs": {
      "roots": [
        { "path": "/opt/app/workspaces/agent-1", "kind": "dir", "access": "rw" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/invoices", "kind": "dir", "access": "ro" },
        { "path": "/data/finance/context.md", "kind": "file", "access": "ro" }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Configure an agent with `tools.fs.roots` containing a writable workspace root and one or more external host paths.
2. Attempt `read`/`write`/`edit`/`apply_patch` and local media access both inside and outside those roots.
3. Attempt outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows with both dir roots and exact file roots.
4. Attempt prompt-image auto-load from a rooted external image path.
5. Attempt the same config in sandbox mode.

### Expected

- Host-mode FS and local media access succeed only within configured roots, respecting `ro` vs `rw`.
- Outbound local-media / reply-delivery flows preserve file-vs-dir root semantics and explicit deny-all behavior.
- Prompt-image auto-load accepts explicitly rooted host paths.
- Sandbox mode ignores `roots` and continues to rely on Docker isolation plus `workspaceOnly` fallback behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, there was no granular host-path policy between workspace-only and unrestricted host access.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`):
  - merged `origin/main` at `631552c554`
  - `pnpm config:docs:gen`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`
  - `pnpm check`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.memory-flush-forwarding.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts`
  - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/media/read-capability.test.ts src/media/local-roots.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "sends transcriptPrompt visibly and queues runtime context as hidden custom context"`
  - `pnpm test src/agents/pi-tools.sandbox-mounted-paths.workspace-only.test.ts -t "ignores host roots for sandboxed read/write/edit tools"`
- Additional focused regressions were exercised during review for:
  - fs-root resolution / precedence
  - alias-safe host mutations and unlink/delete behavior
  - media root propagation and explicit deny-all handling
  - prompt-image and local-media rooted access
  - outbound local-media / reply-delivery adapters
  - pinned-FD host reads (TOCTOU closure)
  - sandbox outbound media ignoring configured roots
- What you did **not** verify on `7b771d448d`:
  - full local `pnpm test`
  - `pnpm build`
  - live end-to-end multi-tenant deployment behavior beyond local seam tests
  - `pnpm check:changed` did not complete locally: it passed type/lint/guard phases and 2,450 tests before a local Vitest gateway worker hit `ERR_WORKER_OUT_OF_MEMORY`

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `tools.fs.roots` from config or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/agents/pi-tools.ts`, `src/agents/tool-fs-policy.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.multi-root-guard.ts`, `src/agents/apply-patch.ts`, media/local-root propagation files, and the config schema/help/generated baseline files.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: host-mode local media or prompt-image access unexpectedly denied for paths inside configured roots, rooted outbound attachments unexpectedly losing exact-file semantics, symlink delete/unlink behavior regressing at root boundaries, or sandbox-generated outbound attachments unexpectedly blocked when host-mode `tools.fs.roots` is configured.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a host-mode path surface could miss roots enforcement and create an unexpected gap.
  - Mitigation: added targeted regression tests for host tools, `apply_patch`, local media inputs, outbound local-media/reply-delivery, and prompt-image auto-load.
- Risk: operator confusion when both `workspaceOnly` and `roots` are present.
  - Mitigation: `roots` precedence is explicit in schema/help and a runtime warning is logged.
- Risk: `exec` still bypasses FS roots through shell commands.
  - Mitigation: documented as an intentional scope boundary; deployments needing hard FS boundaries should not allow `exec`.

## AI assistance

- [x] AI-assisted
- Testing: locally revalidated on the current merged head (`7b771d448d`) with `pnpm check`, focused media/WhatsApp review-thread regressions, and the two prior PR-specific filtered tests; full `pnpm test` and `pnpm build` were not rerun on this final merged head; GitHub CI is green on `7b771d448d` (74 successful, 9 skipped) after the latest review fix.




## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `tools.fs.roots` lets an operator grant an agent narrowly-scoped host filesystem access — an allowlist of roots, each `dir`/`file` with `ro`/`rw` access. Host-mode read/write/edit (and apply_patch) must allow paths inside an allowed root, deny paths outside every root, and reject writes to `ro` roots.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `b4056fc332` (Node 22, macOS), driving the production host-mode filesystem tools against a real on-disk temporary directory tree (real `fs` syscalls; no fixtures or stubs).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Created a real temp tree (an `rw` root `shared-rw`, an `ro` root `shared-ro`, and a `secret/` directory outside every root with a real file in it), built the production tool set via `createOpenClawCodingTools({ config: { tools: { fs: { roots: [...] } } } })`, and executed the resulting `read`/`write` tools against each path with `node --import tsx`. Live console output below.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```text
config tools.fs.roots:
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-rw, kind: dir, access: rw }
  - { path: /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro, kind: dir, access: ro }
workspace (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/workspace
forbidden dir (not a root): /tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret

=== host-mode reads ===
READ  inside rw root  (shared-rw/notes.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "rw-root content"
READ  inside ro root  (shared-ro/policy.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, content: "ro-root content"
READ  OUTSIDE roots   (secret/passwords.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/passwords.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots

=== host-mode writes ===
WRITE inside rw root  (shared-rw/created.txt)
      -> ALLOWED, on disk: true
WRITE inside ro root  (shared-ro/hack.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro/hack.txt' is inside read-only root '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/shared-ro'  (on disk: false)
WRITE OUTSIDE roots   (secret/exfil.txt)
      -> DENIED: Access denied: path '/tmp/fs-roots-proof-qTDt3u/secret/exfil.txt' is outside allowed filesystem roots  (on disk: false)

verify: secret/passwords.txt still says: "TOP SECRET — outside every allowed root"
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Reads inside the `rw` and `ro` roots returned the real file contents. A read of `secret/passwords.txt` (outside every root) was denied with `outside allowed filesystem roots`. A write inside the `rw` root landed on disk (`on disk: true`); a write to the `ro` root was denied (`inside read-only root`) and a write outside every root was denied — both left nothing on disk (`on disk: false`), and the out-of-root secret file was byte-for-byte unchanged afterward.
- **What was not tested:** Sandbox mode is out of scope by design — sandbox ignores `tools.fs.roots` and relies on Docker isolation — so it was not exercised here. No live LLM agent loop was run (no provider key in this environment); the proof drives the exact production tool layer the agent calls, against a real filesystem, rather than a full conversational turn.

target.title: fix(cron): pass allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent from cron executor for both runner paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224: cron jobs that intentionally produce empty replies (silent alert watchers — "watch X, say nothing unless there is something to report") fail every run with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` even when the underlying runner returns success.

Supersedes PR #94491 which used a lane substring heuristic in the shared CLI runner. Per ClawSweeper review, the correct boundary is the cron executor.

## Root cause

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for DM/group chat contexts. The cron executor never sets it, so both the CLI and embedded runner paths throw on empty output.

## Changes

In `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`: set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` on both runner calls:

1. **CLI path** (`runCliAgent` call): added flag
2. **Embedded path** (`runEmbeddedAgent` call): added flag

```typescript
// Intentionally-silent cron jobs (watchers that say nothing unless
// there is something to report) should not throw empty_response.
allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
```

This is a +6 line change to a single file.

## Why this is better than PR #94491

| PR #94491 (old) | This PR (new) |
|----------------|---------------|
| Lane substring check in shared `cli-runner.ts` | Flag set at cron executor boundary |
| Only covers CLI runner path | Covers CLI + embedded runner paths |
| Broadens shared guard for any lane containing "cron" | No shared guard change |
| `!String(lane).includes("cron")` heuristic | Explicit `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Cron jobs with intentionally empty replies fail with `empty_response` error on both CLI and embedded runner paths.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full repository checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```shell
  $ node /tmp/proof-94491-v2.mjs
  ```
  The script verifies the flag propagation for both CLI and embedded paths, DM/chat non-regression, message tool delivery, and text output delivery.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output:
  ```
  ======================================================================
  PR #94491 v2 Live Proof — cron executor allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent
  ======================================================================

  Case 1: CLI runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 2: Embedded runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 3: DM chat (no flag) — empty reply still throws
    result: FailoverError: empty_response
    status: PASS (correctly throws)

  Case 4: Cron job with actual text — normal delivery
    result: All systems nominal.
    status: PASS

  Case 5: Cron job using message tool — delivery handled by tool
    result: ok (message tool handled delivery)
    status: PASS

  Case 6: Verification — executor passes flag to BOTH paths
    CLI path has flag: YES
    Embedded path has flag: YES
    Both paths covered: PASS

  ======================================================================
  RESULTS: 6/6 scenarios pass
  ======================================================================
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Both CLI and embedded cron paths accept empty replies without throwing. DM/group chat contexts are unchanged (still throw on empty replies unless their own `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is set).
- **What was not tested**: Live cron job running on a scheduled Gateway (requires Gateway with cron configuration)

## Related

- PR #94491: original lane-heuristic approach (superseded)
- PR #94235: competing executor implementation for same issue

target.title: fix(agent-tools): resolve workspace tool fs root lazily (#96429)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`openclaw doctor --lint` and `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` crash with:

```
[openclaw] Unhandled promise rejection: FsSafeError: root dir not found
    at resolveRootContext (.../dist/secure-temp-dir-*.js)
    at async root (.../dist/secure-temp-dir-*.js)
```

`doctor --post-upgrade` and other commands are unaffected. Reported in #96429.

Root cause: the doctor `runtime-tool-schemas` check builds the agent coding tools (`createOpenClawCodingTools`) purely to inspect their input schemas. For the workspace-scoped write/edit tools, `createHostWriteOperations` / `createHostEditOperations` eagerly called `fsRoot(workspaceDir)` at tool-construction time:

```ts
const rootPromise = fsRoot(root); // rejects immediately if root is missing
```

When the agent workspace dir does not exist on disk and no filesystem operation is ever invoked (schema inspection only reads `tool.parameters`), that rejected promise is never awaited, so it surfaces as a global unhandled rejection and exits the process. The surrounding `try/catch` only catches synchronous throws, so the async rejection escapes it.

## Why This Change Was Made

The workspace root only needs to be resolved when an fs operation actually runs, not when the tool is constructed. Resolving it lazily removes the floating rejection and is also strictly more correct: the write tool runs `mkdir` before `writeFile`, so a lazy root lets a first write create a not-yet-existing workspace (the eager version rejected before `mkdir` could run).

## User Impact

- `openclaw doctor --lint` and `openclaw doctor --non-interactive` run to completion again instead of crashing when the agent workspace dir is absent.
- No behavior change for existing workspaces; path-boundary enforcement, `mkdir`, and `stat` paths are untouched.

## What Changed

- Added `createLazyWorkspaceRoot(root)`: a memoized resolver that calls `fsRoot(root)` on first use and caches the promise.
- `createHostWriteOperations` and `createHostEditOperations` (workspace-only branches) now resolve the root via this lazy resolver instead of eagerly at construction.
- Added a regression test covering both the no-floated-rejection guarantee and correct resolution on first operation use.

## Evidence

Reproduced the exact failure with a throwaway test before the fix — constructing the two workspace tools against a missing dir (no operation invoked) floated two `FsSafeError: root dir not found` unhandled rejections:

```
+ [
+   FsSafeError { "message": "root dir not found", "code": "not-found", ... },
+   FsSafeError { "message": "root dir not found", "code": "not-found", ... },
+ ]
```

After the fix, the new regression test passes (no floated rejection; first write still creates the workspace and succeeds):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-lazy.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed (2)
```

Existing related suites stay green:

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-edit-access.test.ts \
    src/agents/agent-tools.read.workspace-root-guard.test.ts \
    src/agents/agent-tools.workspace-only-false.test.ts \
    src/agents/agent-tools.read.host-tilde-expansion.test.ts
 Test Files  4 passed (4)
      Tests  38 passed (38)
```

AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(context-engine): avoid quarantining read-only discovery factories
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96335.

OpenClaw can load plugin context-engine registrations while doing read-only plugin discovery. Some plugins, including lossless-claw 0.13.1, intentionally capture that read-only lifecycle in the registered factory and reject stateful engine initialization from that phase. Current main stores that discovery factory like a runtime factory, then `resolveContextEngine()` invokes it, quarantines the selected plugin engine, and falls back to `legacy` for the process.

This PR keeps read-only discovery useful for metadata collection, but prevents discovery-only context-engine factories from being constructed or quarantined as runtime failures.

## Summary

- Adds a context-engine registration lifecycle marker:
  - `runtime`
  - `readOnlyDiscovery`
- Marks plugin API `registerContextEngine()` calls as `runtime` only during full plugin activation.
- Marks discovery/tool-discovery plugin registrations as `readOnlyDiscovery`.
- Prevents read-only discovery registrations from replacing an already-active runtime registration from the same plugin owner.
- Makes `resolveContextEngine()` fall back to the default engine without quarantine when the selected engine is only registered from read-only discovery.
- Keeps runtime factory failures quarantined exactly as before.
- Keeps runtime plugin activation able to replace a prior read-only discovery registration and activate the configured engine.

## Scope Boundary

This intentionally does **not** change the `lightContext` cron contract from #96334. That issue is currently labeled by ClawSweeper as `clawsweeper:no-new-fix-pr`, `clawsweeper:needs-maintainer-review`, and `clawsweeper:needs-product-decision`, so this PR avoids changing that separate product behavior.

## Regression Coverage

Added focused coverage for the lossless-claw-style lifecycle sequence:

1. A plugin-owned context engine is registered from read-only discovery with a factory that throws the reported error: `Engine initialization is disabled during read-only plugin registration`.
2. `resolveContextEngine()` does not invoke that factory.
3. The selected engine is not quarantined.
4. Runtime/full activation replaces the discovery registration and resolves the plugin engine.
5. A later read-only discovery pass does not overwrite the runtime-safe factory.

## Evidence

Local lightweight preflight:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts` — passed, 46 tests.
- `corepack pnpm format src/context-engine/registry.ts src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts src/plugins/registry.ts` — applied formatting to changed files.
- `corepack pnpm format:check src/context-engine/registry.ts src/context-engine/context-engine.test.ts src/plugins/registry.ts` — passed.
- `git diff --check` — passed.

Live / reproducibility notes:

- GitHub Actions for this PR ran on the exact submitted head `0d7cf30c32d2cde431738ba3f46e3ae9640d8902`.
- Official Real behavior proof gate passed: <https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/28089066083/job/83162223916>.
- Configured plugin context-engine proof passed on the exact PR head: <https://github.com/snowzlmbot/openclaw/actions/runs/28093889925>.
  - Artifact: `context-engine-readonly-registration-proof-0d7cf30c32d2cde431738ba3f46e3ae9640d8902`.
  - The proof dynamically creates a real OpenClaw plugin with `kind: ["context-engine"]`, a real `openclaw.plugin.json`, and a runtime file loaded through `loadOpenClawPlugins()`.
  - It configures `plugins.slots.contextEngine = "proof-context-engine"`, then runs a read-only discovery load followed by a full runtime load.
  - Discovery result: `resolvedEngineId=legacy`, `warningCount=1`, `errorCount=0`, and the warning explicitly says the engine is registered for read-only discovery only and falls back without quarantine.
  - Runtime result: `resolvedEngineId=proof-context-engine`; plugin status is `loaded`; `contextEngineIds` contains `proof-context-engine`.
  - Assertions passed: exact head recorded, discovery fallback without quarantine, read-only factory not invoked/quarantined, runtime activation resolves the configured plugin engine, and the runtime registry records the context-engine registration.
- The regression test uses the real OpenClaw context-engine registry/resolve path and reproduces the exact reported read-only initialization error string.
- The public npm registry does not expose `lossless-claw@0.13.1` as `lossless-claw`, so the test cannot install that package directly from npm. The configured-plugin proof reproduces the observed lifecycle failure through OpenClaw's public plugin-owned context-engine registration path.
- A local filtered `src/plugins/loader.test.ts -t "context engine"` run was stopped after it produced no timely output, to avoid turning local preflight into a broad/heavy test run. Broader verification is delegated to GitHub Actions.

## Risk

Low-to-moderate. The change is intentionally narrow around context-engine factory lifecycle handling. The main behavior change is that discovery-only registrations no longer poison runtime resolution through quarantine. If a configured engine never reaches full activation, resolution still falls back to `legacy`, but now it does so without incorrectly quarantining the plugin for the process.

target.title: fix(media-understanding): align video base64 byte limits
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Video max byte limits are compared after estimating the inline base64 payload size, but `resolveVideoMaxBase64Bytes()` converted the configured raw byte limit with `Math.floor(maxBytes * (4 / 3))`. That underestimates any raw byte limit that is not divisible by 3, so a video exactly within the configured raw byte cap can be rejected before provider submission.

For example, `maxBytes = 1` expands to a 4-byte base64 payload, but the previous resolved cap was 1.

## Why This Change Was Made

The helper already has the correct base64 sizing primitive, `estimateBase64Size(bytes)`, which rounds byte counts to complete base64 quanta with `Math.ceil(bytes / 3) * 4`. This change reuses that primitive for the configured max byte limit, keeping the comparison symmetric with the per-file estimate while preserving the shared `DEFAULT_VIDEO_MAX_BASE64_BYTES` cap.

A focused regression test covers the non-multiple-of-three boundaries that previously failed.

## User Impact

Users with custom video byte limits no longer see valid videos rejected only because the expanded base64 limit was rounded down. The global payload cap still applies, so this does not relax the existing maximum request size guard.

## Evidence

Direct boundary probe:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e "import('./packages/media-understanding-common/src/video.ts').then(({ resolveVideoMaxBase64Bytes }) => console.log(JSON.stringify([1, 2, 3, 4].map((maxBytes) => ({ maxBytes, maxBase64Bytes: resolveVideoMaxBase64Bytes(maxBytes) })))))"
[{"maxBytes":1,"maxBase64Bytes":4},{"maxBytes":2,"maxBase64Bytes":4},{"maxBytes":3,"maxBase64Bytes":4},{"maxBytes":4,"maxBase64Bytes":8}]
```

Targeted unit test:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit.config.ts packages/media-understanding-common/src/video.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 C:/Users/lin20/Documents/New project 3/openclaw


 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)
   Start at  21:04:15
   Duration  967ms (transform 682ms, setup 515ms, import 16ms, tests 145ms, environment 0ms)
```

Formatting:

```text
$ node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.cmd --check packages/media-understanding-common/src/video.ts packages/media-understanding-common/src/video.test.ts
Checking formatting...

All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 1235ms on 2 files using 32 threads.
```

Whitespace:

```text
$ git diff --check
```

Prepared with Codex. I reviewed the change, understand the touched code path, and kept the PR focused on the bug described above.

target.title: fix(devices): always route stale-approve operator to openclaw devices list
target.body:
> AI-assisted: drafted with Claude (Opus 4.7). Bug surfaced by the agent
> while operating an OpenClaw 2026.5.7 ECS Fargate deployment in production;
> full transcript available on request.

> **Update (rebased 2026-05-14):** Post-refactor in `fe25ed214e` ("refactor(cli): lazy-load devices runtime"), the
> approve fallback code moved from `src/cli/devices-cli.ts` to `src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts`. This PR's fix
> now applies in the new file; the bug (`return null` silent path → bare `unknown requestId` caller log) is
> still present on main and the diff/tests remain the same shape, just at the new file location.

## Summary

`openclaw devices approve <requestId>` on a loopback gateway has a local
file fallback path (`src/cli/devices-cli.runtime.ts:220-271` (post-refactor; was `src/cli/devices-cli.ts:280-358`)) that approves
directly against `~/.openclaw/devices/{pending.json,paired.json}` when
the CLI cannot dial the gateway as an admin. The fallback compares the
user-supplied `requestId` against `requestId` extracted from the
gateway's pairing-required close error. Three branches today:

1. Gateway error names a different requestId → throw
   `FALLBACK_STATE_MISMATCH_MESSAGE` with `Missing requestId: <gatewayId>`.
   Actionable. ✅
2. Gateway error names the same requestId, but local pending lacks it
   → throw the same state-mismatch error. Actionable. ✅
3. Gateway error names **no** requestId, and local pending lacks the
   user-supplied id → silent `return null`. The caller at the caller line in the runtime file
   only logs `defaultRuntime.error("unknown requestId")`. ❌

Branch 3 fires whenever the gateway emits a `not-paired` close
(`buildPairingConnectErrorMessage` in
`src/gateway/protocol/connect-error-details.ts:275-281` returns the
bare `"pairing required"` string without a parenthetical) **and** the
user-supplied requestId is no longer in `pending.json`. The most common
reason for that disappearance is *supersession*: the gateway dial just
called `requestDevicePairing` → `reconcilePendingPairingRequests` →
`canRefreshSingle` returned false because the incoming and existing
scope sets diverge (`samePendingApprovalSnapshot` requires exact set
equality, `src/infra/device-pairing.ts:314-331`), so the reconciler
**replaced** the entry with a fresh `requestId`. Other causes: the 5
minute TTL (`PENDING_TTL_MS` at `src/infra/device-pairing.ts:132`) or
running approve under a different `OPENCLAW_PROFILE`/`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`.

The operator is left with `unknown requestId` and no pointer to the
current pending state or what to do next.

## Reproduction (before)

ECS Fargate container running `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.5.7`,
Linux x86_64, loopback gateway, paired operator device with only
`operator.read`. Generated a pending request via
`openclaw devices approve --latest` preview (which builds a
scope-upgrade request for the full `CLI_DEFAULT_OPERATOR_SCOPES` set),
then ran the explicit approve:

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11e7be5a-a243-4855-9439-ccc9291449ba --json
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 1dce2124-3c2d-4fa6-98f9-77ccf1e422d1)
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 34a9feb9-847c-48d7-8a42-057c4d3dab82)
unknown requestId
```

`11e7be5a` was the user-supplied id from the preview. The dial built a
new pending whose scope set was a narrower subset of the user-supplied
one (just `operator.pairing` to call `device.pair.approve`); the
reconciler replaced rather than refreshed and `11e7be5a` was gone by
the time the fallback ran. The operator has no way to discover from
this output that the current pending entry exists locally.

## Fix

Replace the silent `return null` with a thrown `Error` whose message
includes:

- the user-supplied (stale) requestId
- a one-line list of plausible causes (supersession during dial, TTL
  prune, profile mismatch)
- a count of other pending entries visible in this profile
- a routing line: `openclaw devices list`

The diagnostic **never prints a direct `Rerun: openclaw devices approve <id>`
command**, even when only one other pending entry is visible. Three
plausible causes (TTL prune, profile/state mismatch, multi-device
profile churn) can each produce a single visible pending that is
unrelated to the operator's intent. The earlier ClawSweeper review
(`P2: Single pending request can be unrelated`) called this out
explicitly. Routing through `openclaw devices list` forces the
operator to verify deviceId + requested scopes before approving,
matching the existing implicit-approve `--latest` flow which is also
preview-only.

No behavior change to the approval semantics in any branch. The other
two error paths (mismatched gatewayRequestId, gatewayRequestId matches
but pending missing) keep the existing `buildFallbackStateMismatchError`
text; only the previously silent `return null` path now throws an
actionable error.

## After

### No other pending entries

```text
$ openclaw devices approve req-stale --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "req-stale" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
No other pending requests are visible in this profile. List pending requests with: openclaw devices list
```

### One other pending entry (still routes to `list` — could be unrelated)

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
1 other pending request is visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

### Multiple other pending entries

```text
$ openclaw devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 --json
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
4 other pending requests are visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

## Tests

Extends `src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts` with three regressions inside the
existing `devices cli local fallback` describe block:

- *"routes the operator to `openclaw devices list` when a single other
  pending request is visible"* — drives the new code path end-to-end
  with exactly one other pending entry. Both `device.pair.list` (the
  precheck scope resolver) and `device.pair.approve` reject with a
  bare `pairing required` message (no requestId),
  `approveDevicePairing` returns `undefined` for the supplied id, and
  a freshly mocked `listDevicePairing` is the only way to surface the
  alternative. Asserts the rejected error contains the
  `1 other pending request is visible` line + the `openclaw devices list`
  routing, **and** asserts the error does NOT contain a `Rerun:` line.
- *"falls back without a fresh requestId hint when no pending entries
  are visible locally"* — same setup but pending list is empty.
  Asserts the error switches to `No other pending requests are visible`.
- *"does not suggest a direct rerun when multiple pending requests are
  visible"* — same setup but two other pending entries. Asserts the
  error routes to `openclaw devices list` AND does not contain a
  `Rerun:` line.

```text
$ pnpm test src/cli/devices-cli.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       32 passed (32)

$ pnpm check:changed
[exit 0]

$ coderabbit review --agent --base origin/main
{"type":"complete","status":"review_completed","findings":0}
```

No changes to `CHANGELOG.md` per contributor convention.

## Why the CLI layer and not the dial/reconciler

I considered two deeper fixes and rejected both as out of scope for a
behaviour-preserving error-surface change:

1. **Preserve the existing requestId across supersession in
   `reconcilePendingPairingRequests`.** `samePendingApprovalSnapshot`
   gating refresh on exact scope-set equality is intentional — the
   replacement branch already merges scopes upward
   (`src/infra/device-pairing.ts:546-558`), so the new pending covers
   the user's intent; only the identifier changes. Allowing refresh on
   superset/subset relationships would change persisted-state
   semantics on every cron's connect handshake, well beyond the bug
   this addresses.
2. **Auto-redirect approval to the gateway-supplied alternative.**
   `approve <id>` is a contract the operator typed explicitly;
   silently approving a different id under the same command would
   surprise scripts and audit trails. The CLI's existing pattern (the
   mismatched-id branch at devices-cli.ts:311-313) chooses error over
   redirect, and this PR keeps that posture. The diagnostic always
   routes through `openclaw devices list` so the operator chooses
   explicitly after seeing deviceId + scopes.

## Why not auto-suggest the single visible pending entry

ClawSweeper flagged this on the first revision of this PR
(`P2: Single pending request can be unrelated`). Earlier revisions of
this fix attempted to print
`Rerun: openclaw devices approve <id>` whenever exactly one alternative
pending request was visible. Counter-examples:

- **TTL prune.** `PENDING_TTL_MS = 5 minutes`. If the operator's stale
  id expired and a different agent (with a different deviceId)
  subsequently created a pending request, the local profile shows one
  pending entry — but it is not the replacement for the stale request.
- **Profile/state-dir mismatch.** The CLI may be looking at a
  different `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` than the gateway. The one visible
  local pending request is local-only and unrelated to the gateway's
  current view.
- **Multi-device profile.** Two agents pairing against the same
  loopback gateway race; either's pending request can be the one
  visible at the moment the fallback runs.

Routing through `openclaw devices list` for the operator to inspect
deviceId + requested scopes before approving is the same safety the
existing `--latest`/implicit-approve preview flow uses
(`src/cli/devices-cli.ts:703+`).

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** `openclaw devices approve <id>` on a
loopback gateway returns the bare error `unknown requestId` whenever
the supplied requestId is missing from local `pending.json` and the
gateway's close error did not carry a requestId in its parenthetical.
The operator has no way to discover from this output that other
pending entries exist or what to do next.

**Real environment tested:**
1. Original bug: live ECS Fargate container running
   `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:2026.5.7` on Linux x86_64, loopback
   gateway with `auth.mode: "token"`, accessed via
   `aws ecs execute-command`. Captured `unknown requestId` from the
   patched-CLI repro path.
2. Patched verification: built the patched CLI from this branch
   (`pnpm build`) and ran it against a local `openclaw gateway --dev`
   instance with an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence`
   on macOS arm64 (Node 22.18.0).

**Steps run after the patch:**

Build the patched CLI, start an isolated dev gateway, auto-pair the CLI
device, stage the bug scenario (downgrade scopes + seed a stale pending
whose scope set is broader than what the dial will ask for), then run
the patched approve:

```sh
cd ~/Projects/public/openclaw
pnpm build

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs gateway --dev --port 18789 --auth none --bind loopback &

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs devices list --token "$TOKEN" --timeout 3000

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr-evidence \
  node ./openclaw.mjs devices approve 11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111 \
    --token "$TOKEN" --timeout 3000 --json
```

A small Python helper between `devices list` and `devices approve` mutates
`devices/paired.json` (downgrade scopes to `operator.read`) and
`devices/pending.json` (insert the stale id with the broader scope set);
omitted here for brevity.

**After-fix evidence (single visible alternative — original bug reproduction):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
1 other pending request is visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

**After-fix evidence (multi-pending case — staged with 4 fake pending entries from different deviceIds):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "11111111-1111-1111-1111-111111111111" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
4 other pending requests are visible in this profile. Inspect device id and requested scopes before approving:
  openclaw devices list
```

**After-fix evidence (no other pending case — also covered by unit regression):**

```text
[openclaw] Reason: Pairing requestId "<id>" is not in the local pending state.
It may have been superseded by a fresh request during the gateway dial, expired (5-minute TTL), or never been created in this profile.
No other pending requests are visible in this profile. List pending requests with: openclaw devices list
```

**Observed result after fix:** The operator always gets a routing to
`openclaw devices list` plus a count of visible pending entries. They
never get a direct `Rerun:` command that could approve an unrelated
device's request. The original ECS-Fargate bug scenario matches the
single-visible-alternative case (after `--latest`-preview-driven
supersession), with the operator now seeing the actionable diagnostic
instead of the dead-end `unknown requestId`.

**What was not tested:** I did not rebuild the OpenClaw container
image with this patch and redeploy it onto the original ECS Fargate
task. The local dev-gateway reproduction stages the exact failure
path (`reconcilePendingPairingRequests` replacing the user-supplied
pending entry during the gateway dial, then the local fallback
running with no matching id), and the patched CLI is the actual
binary built from this branch's commits. A maintainer who wants live
ECS confirmation can stage the patched image, run the two-command
repro above, and read the new error text.

target.title: fix(anthropic): handle max_turns stop reason and surface retry-limit details to users
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat Anthropic `max_turns` as a limit-ended assistant turn, not as a natural model stop.
- Map `max_turns` to the existing normalized `length` stop reason in both Anthropic mapper paths, matching the established `max_tokens` truncation semantics.
- Keep `pause_turn` as ordinary `stop`, and keep the retry-limit user-facing diagnostics added by this PR.
- This update also syncs the branch with current `main` and resolves the test conflict by keeping both the new interleaved-content regression and the existing `max_turns` regression.
- Intentionally does not add a new public stop reason, change retry/failover policy, or touch config, schema, credentials, routing, or non-Anthropic providers.

## Linked context

Closes #78145

## Root cause and scope

- Fix classification: Root-cause fix.
- Root cause: Anthropic can return `max_turns`, but both OpenClaw Anthropic raw-to-normalized stop-reason mappers grouped that provider limit with ordinary `stop` semantics. That made a turn-limit cutoff look like a natural assistant completion instead of a limit-ended assistant turn.
- Why this is root-cause fix: The Anthropic mapper boundary is the source-of-truth boundary where provider stop reasons become OpenClaw's normalized `StopReason`. Mapping `max_turns` to the existing `length` limit-ended reason fixes the invariant at the source for downstream runner completion/retry decisions, rather than masking one caller after the wrong `stop` value has already been emitted.
- Why it matters / User impact: Users and runner logic can distinguish an Anthropic turn-limit cutoff from a natural completion, so partial assistant output is not silently treated as a complete final answer.
- What did NOT change: This PR does not change retry-loop limits, failover policy, timeout thresholds, provider routing, configuration, schema, credentials, transport credentials, non-Anthropic providers, or the public `StopReason` union.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: The canonical contract surface stays the existing normalized `StopReason` union; `length` is reused because it already represents provider/output budget termination (`max_tokens`). `pause_turn` remains `stop`, and only Anthropic's raw `max_turns` normalization changes in the two Anthropic stream paths.
- Patch quality notes: The retry-limit lines in this PR only surface the already-computed retry/timeout message to the user and regression-test that diagnostic text; they do not change retry counts, timeout thresholds, failover decisions, or breaker policy. The public-contract risk is intentionally bounded by reusing `length` instead of adding a new public stop reason.

## Regression test plan

- Target test file: `src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts`; `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts`; `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/retry-limit.test.ts`.
- Scenario locked in: Anthropic emits an SSE `message_delta.delta.stop_reason` of `max_turns` after partial text and usage. OpenClaw returns `stopReason: "length"` while preserving the partial text and token usage in both Anthropic stream implementations.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The regression tests exercise the two mapper boundaries where the bad raw provider value was normalized; the retry-limit tests only lock the related user-visible diagnostics so this PR does not rely on caller-side fallback behavior.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Anthropic `max_turns` responses were being normalized as `stop`, which makes a turn-limit cutoff look like a natural model completion. The fix makes both Anthropic code paths report `length` while preserving partial text and usage.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node v24.16.0 and the repository package environment. The runtime proof directly invoked OpenClaw's production `streamAnthropic` function against an Anthropic-format SSE `Response`, so it exercised the real provider stream parser and stop-reason mapper without making a live API call.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  pnpm exec tsx --eval '<script imports streamAnthropic, feeds stop_reason=max_turns, prints normalized_stop_reason, and exits nonzero unless the result is length>'
  ```
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Console output from the runtime stream proof:
  ```text
  Anthropic max_turns runtime stream proof
  raw_stop_reason=max_turns
  normalized_stop_reason=length
  content=[{"type":"text","text":"Partial turn"}]
  usage={"input":4,"output":8,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"totalTokens":12,"cost":{"input":0,"output":0,"cacheRead":0,"cacheWrite":0,"total":0}}
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The production Anthropic provider stream path consumed a `message_delta` with `stop_reason: "max_turns"`, preserved the partial assistant text (`Partial turn`) and token usage, and returned `normalized_stop_reason=length` instead of `stop`.
- What was not tested: A live Anthropic API run that actually exhausts the upstream turn budget, because forcing `max_turns` from the hosted API on demand is not deterministic.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The issue-specific runtime proof replays an Anthropic-format SSE response through OpenClaw's real provider stream code instead of waiting for a hosted Anthropic API budget exhaustion event.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
  ```text
  FAIL  Anthropic provider > maps Anthropic max_turns as a length-limited stop reason
  AssertionError: expected 'stop' to be 'length'
  Expected: "length"
  Received: "stop"

  FAIL  anthropic transport stream > maps Anthropic max_turns as a length-limited stop reason
  AssertionError: expected 'stop' to be 'length'
  Expected: "length"
  Received: "stop"

  Test Files  3 failed (3)
       Tests  3 failed | 183 skipped (186)
  ```

## Tests and validation

Runtime proof command:
```bash
pnpm exec tsx --eval '<script imports streamAnthropic, feeds stop_reason=max_turns, and verifies normalized_stop_reason=length>'
```

Original focused regression command:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts -t "maps Anthropic max_turns as a length-limited stop reason"
```

Post-sync focused coverage command:
```bash
corepack pnpm@11.2.2 exec node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/retry-limit.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

Regression coverage added:
- `src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts` covers Anthropic `message_delta.delta.stop_reason: "max_turns"` in the provider path.
- `src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts` covers the same stop reason in the transport-stream path.
- Both tests assert the normalized stop reason is `length` and that partial content plus usage are preserved.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/retry-limit.test.ts` covers the user-visible retry-limit diagnostic text.

The new regression failed before the mapper change with `Received: "stop"`, then passed after mapping `max_turns` to `length` in both Anthropic mappers.

Validation results:
```text
Runtime stream proof: normalized_stop_reason=length
Original focused regression: Test Files 3 passed (3), Tests 3 passed | 183 skipped (186)
Post-sync focused coverage:
  [test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 29.97s
  unit-fast: src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/retry-limit.test.ts — 4 passed
  unit: src/llm/providers/anthropic.test.ts — 37 passed
  agents: src/agents/anthropic-transport-stream.test.ts — 78 passed
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`) Anthropic turn-limit cutoffs are no longer reported as ordinary natural stops.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- Stop-reason classification can affect runner completion/retry decisions for Anthropic sessions that hit a provider turn limit.

How is that risk mitigated?
- The fix reuses the existing normalized `length` reason already used for limit-ended output (`max_tokens`) instead of adding a new public API value.
- The regression tests cover both Anthropic mapper paths and assert content/usage preservation, so only the completion classification changes.
- The conflict resolution preserved current `main`'s interleaved active-content block test alongside the `max_turns` test, so the stream parser's index-routing behavior remains covered after sync.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Ready for maintainer review after this update lands on the PR branch and remote checks rerun.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- No known author-side code or local validation action remains. A live hosted Anthropic `max_turns` exhaustion event was not forced because it is not deterministic on demand; the runtime proof uses a provider-shaped SSE response through OpenClaw's production stream code.
- Maintainers still own the semantic decision called out by ClawSweeper: whether `max_turns` should reuse the existing normalized `length` terminal state rather than introducing distinct metadata or user-facing text.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
- RF-001: Current tests prove both Anthropic mapper paths normalize `max_turns` to `length` while preserving partial content and usage. The remaining question is the maintainer semantic acceptance of `length` as the normalized provider-limit state.
- RF-002: The PR intentionally treats `max_turns` as defensive observed/compatible stream handling rather than a new public Anthropic SDK contract; the scope remains limited to raw provider stop-reason normalization.
- RF-003: The real behavior proof request is addressed with copied runtime terminal output showing `raw_stop_reason=max_turns` becoming `normalized_stop_reason=length`, plus RED/GREEN regression evidence for both Anthropic mapper paths.

target.title: fix(memory-core): keep short protected-glossary terms past the min-length gate
target.body:
## Summary

`PROTECTED_GLOSSARY` (`extensions/memory-core/src/concept-vocabulary.ts`) exists specifically to preserve short technical terms that the generic stop-word/segmentation filtering would otherwise discard. But every glossary match still flows through `normalizeConceptToken` (via `pushNormalizedTag`), which applies a per-script minimum length — latin tokens shorter than 3 chars are rejected. The glossary lists `kv` and `s3` (2-char latin), so they were **never** emitted as concept tags despite being on the protect-list, defeating the allowlist.

`deriveConceptTags` is called from short-term recall/promotion (`short-term-promotion.ts`) for entries without stored `conceptTags`, so any recalled snippet mentioning these terms lost them.

### Changes
- `concept-vocabulary.ts` — thread a `fromGlossary` flag through `pushNormalizedTag` → `normalizeConceptToken` that bypasses **only** the min-length gate for glossary matches; all other gates (empty/non-letter, oversized, pure-number, date, stop-word) still apply. `deriveConceptTags` passes `fromGlossary: true` for glossary matches, `false` for compound/segment tokens.
- `concept-vocabulary.test.ts` — regression test that `kv`/`s3` survive.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: 2-char latin glossary terms (`kv`, `s3`) were silently dropped by the latin min-length-3 gate. After the fix they are emitted as concept tags.

Real environment tested: Node 22.22.1, macOS, no network/model. Vitest exercises the real exported `deriveConceptTags`. BEFORE is `origin/main`'s file (swapped via `git show`).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/concept-vocabulary.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:
```
deriveConceptTags({ path: "memory/2026-04-04.md", snippet: "Store the session in kv and back up to s3 nightly." })
  BEFORE: tags do NOT contain "kv" or "s3"   (dropped by the latin min-length-3 gate)
  AFTER:  tags contain "kv" and "s3"          (glossary allowlist honored)
```

Observed result after fix: short protected-glossary terms survive; 3-char+ latin entries, CJK entries, and all non-glossary filtering are unchanged. The new test fails on `origin/main` and passes after.

What was not tested: end-to-end short-term recall promotion (the test drives the real exported `deriveConceptTags` that path calls). Additive only — it surfaces two previously-dropped glossary tags and never removes or reorders existing tags.

## Additional checks
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/concept-vocabulary.test.ts` passes; fail-before verified by swapping in `origin/main`'s file. Extension `oxlint` clean.

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix: warn when configured channel plugins cannot load
target.body:
Related: #91873

## What Problem This Solves

Addresses the gateway-startup visibility part of #91873: when a configured external channel is present but its channel plugin is not loadable under the current plugin trust/allow policy, the gateway can otherwise print a normal ready banner while the channel is absent from the loaded plugin list.

## Why This Change Was Made

The change keeps the explicit plugin trust boundary intact and makes the failure mode visible during gateway startup. Startup logging now uses the raw activation source config for channel-plugin trust checks, so runtime-only auto-enable state does not hide an unresolved `plugins.allow`/`plugins.entries` problem.

## User Impact

Operators now get a startup warning when a configured channel has no loadable channel plugin. The warning names the configured channel and points to the plugin enablement/trust fix instead of leaving the channel silently absent.

## Evidence

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_FS_MODULE_CACHE_PATH=.vitest-cache-91873 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=300000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-log.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts src/gateway/server-import-boundary.test.ts`  
  Result: 6 files passed, 128 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/gateway/server.impl.ts src/gateway/server-startup-log.ts src/gateway/server-startup-log.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`  
  Result: all matched files use the correct format.
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/gateway/server.impl.ts src/gateway/server-startup-log.ts src/gateway/server-startup-log.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.test.ts`  
  Result: passed.
- `git diff --check`  
  Result: passed.
- Real gateway startup proof with a temporary installed `proof-chat` channel plugin and two isolated configs: one blocked by `plugins.allow=["browser"]`, one trusted with `plugins.entries.proof-chat.enabled=true`.  
  Result excerpt:
  - blocked: `http server listening (1 plugin: browser; 0.8s)`
  - blocked: `configured channel warning: channels.proof-chat: channel is configured, but plugin "proof-chat" is installed but omitted from plugins.allow. Include "proof-chat" in plugins.allow. Fix plugin enablement before relying on setup guidance for this channel.`
  - trusted: `http server listening (1 plugin: proof-chat; 0.8s)`
  - trusted: no `configured channel warning: channels.proof-chat`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`  
  Result: first run found a missing-owner regression; after fixing that fallback, final run was clean with no accepted/actionable findings.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Gateway startup now logs a warning when a configured channel has no loadable channel plugin under the startup activation policy, including restrictive `plugins.allow` cases.

Real environment tested: Local source checkout on macOS with the repo CLI, using isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` values.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Installed a temporary local channel plugin with `pnpm openclaw plugins install <temp proof plugin> --force`; started `pnpm openclaw gateway run --port <temp port> --bind loopback --auth token --token proof-token --force --allow-unconfigured` once with `plugins.allow=["browser"]` and `channels.proof-chat` configured, then once with `plugins.entries.proof-chat.enabled=true`; also ran the focused Vitest, oxfmt, oxlint, `git diff --check`, and autoreview commands listed above.

Evidence after fix: The blocked gateway run reached the ready banner with only `browser` loaded and then emitted the configured-channel warning for `channels.proof-chat`; the trusted gateway run reached the ready banner with `proof-chat` loaded and emitted no configured-channel warning.

Observed result after fix: A configured-but-unloadable channel no longer disappears behind only the normal ready banner; startup emits a warning with the configured channel id and remediation path, while a trusted configured channel does not false-positive.

What was not tested: I did not run a live Slack Socket Mode upgrade or external Slack API connection; this PR proves the gateway startup warning and activation-policy behavior with a real local gateway process and a synthetic channel plugin.
